rationale: Init uses \in for state/flag then immediately constrains them with \A, which is redundant and confusing. The flag variable is never used as a guard - it is set/unset but does not influence transitions. Turn never advances, so only one process can ever enter critical. TypeOK is misnamed - it is actually the mutex invariant, not a type invariant. Unused EXTENDS (Sequences, FiniteSets). Would teach bad naming and redundant variable patterns.

Cleaner structure than spec 0 with proper Init. However, turn never advances so only one process can ever enter critical section - this models mutual exclusion trivially by starving all but one process. TypeOK mixes type invariant with safety property. Mutex is correct but the algorithm is not useful as a real mutual exclusion protocol.

Nearly identical to spec 1 with minor formatting differences. Same fundamental issue: turn never advances, trivially enforcing mutex by starvation. Having near-duplicate specs in training data provides no additional value and would waste training signal.
Another near-duplicate of specs 1-2 with only cosmetic difference (uses /= instead of #). Same static-turn flaw. Redundant with other variants; adds no training diversity.

Yet another near-duplicate of specs 1-3. Invariant uses disjunction form (state[p] # critical \/ state[q] # critical) instead of negated conjunction, but semantically identical. No new training value.
Exact duplicate of spec 1 in structure and invariant form. Provides zero additional training value. The static-turn problem remains.

Only has TypeOK as invariant - completely missing the mutual exclusion safety property, which is the entire point of the spec. This would teach a model that TypeOK alone is sufficient for a mutex spec. Same static-turn flaw as others. Dangerous training example.

Better algorithm: checks no other process is critical before entering (guard-based mutex). However, UNCHANGED <<>> is incorrect TLA+ (empty tuple). Init pattern uses \in then constrains with \A, which is redundant. TypeOK mixes type and safety invariant. The UNCHANGED bug would cause TLC errors.

Guard-based mutex like spec 7. Uses explicit frame condition on primed variables (state'[j] = state[j]) which is non-idiomatic - EXCEPT already handles this. Same redundant Init pattern. The conjunction of frame condition with disjunctive actions may cause unexpected behavior.

Uses CONSTANTS for state enum values (Idle, Trying, Critical) which requires model value assignment and is non-idiomatic. Turn advances on exit (good fairness property). However, only 3 distinct states with N processes suggests the model is highly constrained. Missing explicit mutex invariant - only has TypeOK. Would teach unusual constant-as-enum pattern.

Cleanest mutex spec so far: proper UNCHANGED usage, turn advances on exit for fairness. However, only TypeOK invariant with no mutual exclusion safety property - the core purpose of the spec is unverified. Good idiomatic TLA+ but missing the key invariant makes it a poor training example for property specification.

Fundamentally broken: the trying state only sets turn' = p without changing state to critical. There is no transition from trying to critical, so processes get permanently stuck in trying. Only TypeOK invariant. Would teach incorrect state machine patterns.

The UNCHANGED turn is at the wrong scope level - it only applies to the last disjunct due to TLA+ operator precedence, leaving turn unspecified in the first two transitions. This is a subtle but critical bug. Only TypeOK invariant. Would teach incorrect understanding of TLA+ conjunction/disjunction precedence.

Confusing precedence: first disjunct specifies turn' = turn while UNCHANGED turn is conjoined at outer level. The conjunction of turn' = turn (from first disjunct) with UNCHANGED turn (outer) is redundant but not wrong. However, turn=1 never changes so only process 1 can enter critical - trivial mutex by starvation. Only TypeOK invariant.

Uses %(turn, N) which is not valid TLA+ syntax - % is not a TLA+ operator (should be turn % N or modular arithmetic via Integers). Skips the trying state entirely (idle->critical). Only TypeOK invariant. Would teach invalid TLA+ syntax and an incorrect state machine.

Fundamentally broken: Next allows any process to transition to any state (trying, critical, idle) without guards. Multiple processes can be in critical section simultaneously. TypeOK invariant is redundant (second conjunct restates first). No mutex property. Would teach completely wrong specification patterns.

Turn advances linearly (turn+1) with guard turn'<=N, meaning the system deadlocks after N-1 steps when turn reaches N. No trying state modeled. Only 3 distinct states for N processes. TypeOK only. Fundamentally broken finite-run algorithm.
Identical to spec 16 with only formatting differences. Same linear turn advancement bug causing deadlock. Duplicate provides no value.

Clean guard-based mutex with single state variable. Correctly checks no other process is critical before entering. Good idiomatic TLA+. However, only TypeOK invariant - the mutual exclusion property is enforced by the guards but never stated as an invariant to verify. Missing Spec definition in the module body (it exists but after ====).
chain_of_thought: The problem requires mutual exclusion for N processes, so the core state variable is a function mapping each process in 1..N to its current phase: idle, trying, or critical. This three-valued enum is necessary and sufficient because the protocol has exactly three observable phases per process -- not yet competing, requesting entry, and inside the critical section. The state space is bounded because each of N processes independently occupies one of three discrete states, giving 3^N reachable configurations. The idle-to-trying transition models a process announcing intent to enter; the trying-to-critical transition models actual entry, guarded by the conjunct that no other process is already critical (this is where mutual exclusion is enforced at the action level); the critical-to-idle transition models release. The TypeOK invariant restricts the state function's range, while the implicit safety property -- at most one process in critical -- follows directly from the guard on Enter: a process can only move to critical when all others are not critical, so two simultaneous critical states are unreachable from Init under the Next relation.

rationale: Minimal guard-based mutex identical to spec 18 but without even Spec definition or comments. Correct algorithm but only TypeOK invariant. Too terse for a good training example - no comments, no Spec definition, no safety property. Duplicate of spec 18.

Critically broken: StartEating and StopEating assign primed variables then also have UNCHANGED <<philosophers, forks>>, creating contradictions that make these actions impossible. Invariants are confused: NoForkConflict asserts eating philosophers cannot have free forks (backwards logic - should check held forks). NoForkConflictStop is an exact duplicate of NoForkConflict. Would teach contradictory action patterns.

Good overall structure with proper state machine, helper functions, and meaningful invariants (TypeOK + Safety + Liveness). However, UNCHANGED <<>> in PickUp and PutDown is invalid TLA+ syntax that would cause TLC errors. Safety property correctly prevents adjacent eating. leftFork helper uses i-1 convention (philosopher's left fork is the one to their left) which is reasonable. Despite the UNCHANGED bug, this is one of the better structured specs.
chain_of_thought: The dining philosophers problem requires two state variables: philosophers (a function 1..N -> {thinking, hungry, eating}) tracking each philosopher's phase, and forks (a function 1..N -> BOOLEAN) tracking whether each shared fork is available. Two variables are needed because fork availability is not derivable from philosopher states alone -- a philosopher becomes hungry before acquiring forks, so state and resource ownership are decoupled. The circular topology is encoded by leftFork(i) and rightFork(i) using modular arithmetic wrapping 1-to-N and N-to-1. The state space is bounded: N philosophers each in 3 states times N forks each in 2 states gives 3^N * 2^N configurations. Three actions model the real-world events: Hungry(i) transitions thinking-to-hungry without touching forks; PickUp(i) atomically acquires both adjacent forks when free, transitioning hungry-to-eating and marking forks FALSE; PutDown(i) releases both forks, transitioning eating-to-thinking. The Safety invariant states no two adjacent philosophers eat simultaneously, which is sufficient because PickUp requires both shared forks to be free, and adjacent eaters would require the same fork to be held by two philosophers -- impossible since forks are set to FALSE atomically on acquisition.

rationale: Well-structured spec with fork ownership tracking (0=free, p=owned). Clean action decomposition (Hungry/Eat/Think). TypeOK has redundant conjuncts. Missing explicit safety invariant - no 'no adjacent eating' property stated. Forks/States definitions as operators rather than initial values is slightly unusual but works. One of the better dining philosopher specs despite missing safety property.
chain_of_thought: This variant of dining philosophers uses an ownership-tracking encoding for forks: forks is a function 1..N -> 0..N where 0 means free and a positive value p means philosopher p holds that fork. This is richer than a Boolean encoding because it lets the spec directly assert which philosopher owns which fork, enabling stronger type invariants. The states variable tracks each philosopher in {thinking, hungry, eating}. LeftFork and RightFork use the standard circular index wrapping. The Hungry action models a philosopher declaring intent without acquiring resources. The Eat action is guarded by both adjacent forks being 0 (free), then atomically sets both fork entries to the philosopher's ID and transitions to eating -- this atomic two-fork acquisition avoids partial-grab deadlock at the specification level. The Think action requires the philosopher to currently own both forks (verified via forks[LeftFork(p)] = p), then releases them by resetting to 0. The state space is bounded by N * (N+1)^N * 3^N. TypeOK captures this structure, ensuring fork values stay in 0..N and states in the three-element set.

rationale: Hardcoded N=5 instead of using CONSTANT reduces generality. Fork ownership model is unusual: forks[i]=i means 'free' (owned by home position). Thinking->hungry transition simultaneously grabs forks, combining two logical steps. TypeOK is misleading - checks fork ownership consistency rather than types. Missing proper safety invariant for adjacent eating.

Decent state machine structure with clean string-based fork states. However, NoAdjacentEating invariant is logically backwards: it asserts that eating implies adjacent forks are FREE, when they should be OCCUPIED during eating. This invariant would actually fail on correct behavior. Hardcoded N=5 reduces generality. Would teach incorrect invariant writing.

Well-structured spec with CONSTANT N, clean action decomposition, and proper helper predicates. Fork ownership model (forks[i]=i means free) is clear. Includes weak fairness for liveness. However, only TypeOK invariant - missing explicit safety property (no adjacent eating). PutDown correctly restores fork ownership. One of the better dining philosopher specs.
chain_of_thought: This dining philosophers variant uses a self-ownership convention for free forks: forks[i] = i means fork i is free (owned by its home position), while forks[i] = j for j != i means philosopher j holds it. This avoids introducing 0 as a sentinel. The CanEat(i) predicate checks that both the left fork (indexed i) and right fork (indexed Right(i)) are at their home positions. PickUp atomically claims both forks by setting them to i and transitions to eating. PutDown returns each fork to its home position (forks[i] = i, forks[Right(i)] = Right(i)) and transitions to thinking. BecomeHungry transitions thinking-to-hungry without modifying forks. The state space is bounded: forks ranges over [1..N -> 1..N] and states over [1..N -> 3 values], giving N^N * 3^N states. The weak fairness WF condition on Next ensures liveness -- every continuously enabled action eventually fires, preventing indefinite starvation. Mutual exclusion of adjacent eaters is guaranteed because PickUp requires both forks at home positions, and any eating philosopher has moved those forks away from home.

rationale: Has a fork leak bug: thinking->hungry grabs forks, but 4th action (hungry->thinking) does not release them (forks'=forks). Also the 4th action's guard checks ForkFree which contradicts the philosopher already holding forks from the first transition. Hardcoded N=5. TypeOK only checks forks range, no safety property. Confused state machine with contradictory transitions.

Uses %(i, N)+1 which is not valid TLA+ syntax - % is not a TLA+ operator. This would fail to parse. TypeOK has redundant conjuncts. Otherwise decent structure with proper action decomposition, but the syntax error makes it completely unusable. Would teach invalid TLA+ syntax.

Reasonably clean spec with string-based fork states. State machine is mostly correct: hungry->eating grabs forks, eating->thinking releases them. Thinking->hungry has unnecessary guard on forks[i]='free'. Hardcoded N=5 reduces generality. Has TypeOK but no explicit safety property for adjacent eating. Forks don't track which philosopher holds them, limiting fork-conflict detection.

Correct state machine with fork ownership tracking. However, uses integer constants for states (0,1,2) which is non-idiomatic TLA+ - strings are standard and more readable. Hardcoded N=5. Missing safety invariant. Only TypeOK. The integer-state pattern would teach non-standard TLA+ conventions.

Models power states and battery well with valid transitions. However, invariants are weak: SafetyValid and SafetyBounded are just restatements of TypeOK. No meaningful safety property beyond typing (e.g., 'cannot be active with zero battery'). Using integers 0-3 instead of string constants for states is non-idiomatic.
chain_of_thought: The problem specifies four device states (off, sleep, active, charging) with valid transitions between them, so we need a `state` variable encoded as integers 0-3 to track the current power mode, plus a `battery` variable because charging and draining require tracking energy level to determine when transitions are possible (e.g., cannot turn on with zero battery). The state space is bounded: state ranges over {0,1,2,3} and battery over 0..3, giving exactly 16 reachable configurations. Each action models a specific hardware event: TurnOn powers up from off (requires battery > 0), Sleep/Wake toggle between active and sleep, TurnOff shuts down from active or sleep, StartCharge/StopCharge enter and exit charging mode (only from off), Charge increments battery, and Drain decrements battery during active use. The safety invariants TypeOK, SafetyValid, and SafetyBounded collectively ensure the device is always in one of the four known states and battery remains within physical bounds. These are sufficient because every action's guard restricts the source state and the primed values stay within the declared domains, so no reachable state can violate the known-state requirement from the problem description.

rationale: Good simplified Raft log model. SafetyValid captures the key ordering invariant (commitIdx <= followerLog <= leaderLog). Completeness limited by single follower and no term/leader election, but appropriate for the stated scope. Clean TLA+ idioms with CONSTANTs and proper action structure.
chain_of_thought: The Raft log replication protocol requires three state variables: `leaderLog` tracks how many entries the leader has appended (the source of truth), `followerLog` tracks how many the follower has replicated (must lag behind leader), and `commitIdx` tracks the commit point (entries safe to apply, must lag behind follower replication). The state space is bounded by the constant Max which caps leaderLog, and the ordering invariant followerLog <= leaderLog, commitIdx <= followerLog constrains the other two. Three actions model the protocol: AppendEntry represents the leader receiving a client request and appending to its log, Replicate models the follower catching up by copying one entry at a time (guarded by followerLog < leaderLog), and Commit advances the commit index once the follower has replicated (guarded by commitIdx < followerLog), modeling the majority-acknowledgment step. The safety invariant SafetyValid (followerLog <= leaderLog /\ commitIdx <= followerLog) derives from the Raft protocol requirement that followers never lead the leader and commits never exceed replication. This invariant is sufficient because it establishes a monotonic chain 0 <= commitIdx <= followerLog <= leaderLog <= Max, ensuring no entry is committed before replication and no replication exceeds the leader's log.

rationale: Trivially simple: a single integer counter going up and down. All four invariants (TypeOK, SafetyBounded, NoOverflow, NoUnderflow) express the exact same property. No controller logic, no setpoints, no hysteresis. Would teach nothing beyond a counter pattern.

Correctly models inbox with unread/read partition and bounded capacity. SafetyBounded is a meaningful invariant (total messages within capacity). However, missing delete-unread action and no notion of message identity. Reasonably clean TLA+ but invariants could be stronger (e.g., monotonicity of total received).
chain_of_thought: The email inbox needs to distinguish between unread and already-read messages because the read operation converts one to the other, so we need two state variables `unread` and `read` rather than a single total count. The state space is bounded by the constant Max which caps the total messages (unread + read <= Max), yielding at most (Max+1)^2 states. Three actions model the inbox operations: Receive adds a new unread message (guarded by capacity), ReadMsg transfers one message from unread to read (decrementing unread, incrementing read atomically), and DeleteRead removes a read message permanently. The safety invariants SafetyBounded (unread + read <= Max) and SafetyValid (both non-negative) follow directly from the physical constraints of bounded storage and non-negative counts. These invariants are sufficient because Receive's guard prevents overflow, ReadMsg's transfer preserves the total, and DeleteRead only decreases it, so no sequence of actions can violate capacity or produce negative counts.

rationale: The artificial bound nextId < Capacity * 2 is unmotivated and limits the spec unnecessarily. There is no actual FIFO modeling -- no sequence, no ordering guarantee. All three invariants say the same thing (chanLen within bounds). Does not teach FIFO semantics despite the name.

Correct conservation model. ResourceConserved (available + inUse = N) is the key safety property and is well-stated. Clean and idiomatic. However, it is structurally identical to many other specs in this batch (counter pair summing to constant), reducing its marginal training value.
chain_of_thought: The resource pool has a fixed total of N resources partitioned between available and in-use, requiring two state variables `available` and `inUse` because each checkout moves a resource from one partition to the other and we must track both sides to enforce conservation. The state space is bounded with each variable in 0..N and the invariant available + inUse = N further constraining reachable states to exactly N+1 configurations. Checkout models a client acquiring a resource (guarded by available > 0 to prevent underflow, atomically decrements available and increments inUse), while Return models releasing it back (guarded by inUse > 0, reverse transfer). The key safety invariant ResourceConserved (available + inUse = N) derives directly from the requirement that total resources remain constant, and it is an inductive invariant because both Checkout and Return transfer exactly one unit between the two variables. Combined with SafetyInv ensuring non-negativity, these invariants guarantee no resource is created or destroyed and the pool never enters an inconsistent state.

rationale: Well-modeled circuit breaker with all three states and proper transitions. SafetyInv (Open => failures >= Threshold) is a meaningful invariant. The inline IF in the Failure action is slightly awkward but functional. Good training example for state machine patterns with threshold-based transitions.
chain_of_thought: The circuit breaker pattern requires two state variables: `state` (a string in {Closed, Open, HalfOpen}) to track the breaker's mode, and `failures` (an integer) to count consecutive failures that trigger the transition from Closed to Open. The state space is bounded because state has exactly 3 values and failures ranges over 0..Threshold, giving 3*(Threshold+1) states. Five actions model the pattern: Success resets the failure counter in Closed state, Failure increments failures and conditionally transitions to Open when the threshold is reached, Trip moves from Open to HalfOpen (modeling a timeout/probe attempt), HalfSuccess closes the breaker on a successful probe, and HalfFailure reopens it on a failed probe. The safety invariant (state = Open => failures >= Threshold) derives from the requirement that the breaker only opens after accumulating enough failures, and FailuresBounded ensures the counter never exceeds Threshold. These invariants are sufficient because the Failure action is the only path to Open state and it sets failures to at least Threshold, while HalfFailure preserves the failure count, so the implication holds inductively across all reachable states.

rationale: Models only a counter of keys, not actual key-value semantics. lastOp variable adds no meaningful state -- it is never used in guards or invariants. All invariants just say keyCount is bounded. Misleadingly named: teaches nothing about KV store semantics (no keys, no values, no conflicts).

The requests counter with Max*2 bound is artificial and serves no modeling purpose. Token bucket semantics are incomplete -- refill adds one token at a time with no notion of time windows. All invariants just bound the token count. Does not model the relationship between time and token replenishment.

Correctly models job lifecycle with concurrency bound (running <= Max). AtMostMaxRunning is a meaningful invariant. The unbounded submit (up to Max*2 total jobs) is somewhat arbitrary. Missing job identity and priority, but acceptable for the abstraction level. Decent training example for concurrent resource bounding.
chain_of_thought: The job scheduler requires three state variables `pending`, `running`, and `done` to track jobs in each lifecycle phase, because the concurrency constraint applies specifically to running jobs and we need to distinguish jobs that have completed from those still queued. The state space is bounded: running is capped at Max (the concurrency limit), while pending and done are bounded by Max*2 (an upper bound on total jobs ever submitted). Submit models job arrival (guarded by total capacity), Start moves a pending job to running (guarded by both pending > 0 and running < Max to enforce the concurrency limit), and Complete moves a running job to done. The critical safety invariant AtMostMaxRunning (running <= Max) derives directly from the concurrency requirement, and it holds inductively because Start is the only action that increments running and its guard running < Max prevents exceeding the limit. SafetyInv ensures all counters remain non-negative, which holds because each decrement action guards on the counter being positive.

rationale: Good model of a TTL-based lock with acquire, tick, expire, and release. SafetyInv (unlocked => ttl=0) is meaningful and non-trivial. The TTL countdown pattern is useful for training. Minor issue: only models one lock holder, no contention. Clean idiomatic TLA+.
chain_of_thought: A distributed lock with TTL requires two state variables: `locked` (boolean) to represent whether the lock is held, and `ttl` (integer) to model the time-to-live countdown that enables automatic expiration, which is the core mechanism distinguishing this from a simple lock. The state space is bounded with locked in BOOLEAN and ttl in 0..MaxTTL, giving 2*(MaxTTL+1) states. Four actions model the lock lifecycle: Acquire sets locked=TRUE and ttl=MaxTTL (only when unlocked), TickTTL decrements the TTL while the lock is held and ttl > 1, Expire releases the lock when TTL reaches 1 or below (modeling automatic timeout), and Release models explicit unlock by the holder. The key safety invariant (locked = FALSE => ttl = 0) captures the consistency requirement that an unlocked state must have zero remaining TTL, derived from the physical meaning that TTL only exists for an active lock. This invariant is sufficient because Acquire always sets both locked=TRUE and ttl=MaxTTL together, while both Expire and Release atomically set locked=FALSE and ttl=0, so there is no reachable state where the lock is free but TTL is positive.

rationale: Models cache hits/misses/eviction at counter level. The Capacity*3 bound on hits+misses is arbitrary. MissEvict action (miss without adding entry when full) is a nice touch. But invariants are weak -- just bounding entry count. No eviction policy modeled (LRU, FIFO, etc.), which is the interesting part of cache eviction.

Clean pipeline model with boolean stages and proper advancement guards. However, invariants are weak -- just bounding the produced counter. Missing the key pipeline invariant (e.g., items only move forward, no stage is occupied unless upstream has processed). The produced counter is not closely tied to pipeline correctness.
chain_of_thought: A 3-stage pipeline where each stage holds at most one item requires boolean variables `s1`, `s2`, `s3` for stage occupancy (booleans suffice since capacity is exactly one), plus an integer `produced` to bound the total items entering the pipeline and ensure finite state space. The state space is 2^3 * (Max+1) states, bounded by the boolean stage variables and the Max constant. Four actions model the pipeline flow: Enter places an item in stage 1 (guarded by s1=FALSE and produced < Max), Advance12 moves an item from stage 1 to 2 (requires s1=TRUE and s2=FALSE to prevent collision), Advance23 similarly moves from stage 2 to 3, and Exit removes the completed item from stage 3. The advancement guards (destination must be empty) enforce the single-occupancy invariant structurally, meaning no explicit invariant is needed to prevent two items in one stage. SafetyInv and ProducedBounded ensure produced never exceeds Max, which holds because Enter is the only action incrementing produced and its guard prevents exceeding Max. The pipeline ordering is enforced by the boolean handshake pattern: an item cannot advance until the next stage is free.

rationale: The mode variable is set but never used in any guard -- the controller does not actually use mode to decide whether to heat or cool. No hysteresis, no setpoint, no deadband. Both invariants say the same thing (temp bounded). This is just a counter with a label, not a controller. Would teach bad modeling habits.

Captures basic auction flow. SafetyInv (closed => bidCount > 0) is meaningful -- ensures auction only closes after at least one bid. However, bids always increment by 1 which is unrealistic. No multiple bidders, no notion of highest bidder identity. currentBid and bidCount are always equal, making one redundant.
chain_of_thought: The auction system requires three state variables: `currentBid` to track the highest bid amount (must monotonically increase), `bidCount` to track how many bids have been placed, and `auctionOpen` (boolean) to control whether bidding is permitted. The state space is bounded by Max for both integer variables and the boolean for auction state, giving (Max+1)^2 * 2 configurations. PlaceBid models a new bid that exceeds the current highest (simplified as incrementing by 1, ensuring monotonic increase) while the auction is open, and CloseAuction models ending the auction (guarded by bidCount > 0 to ensure at least one bid exists before closing). The safety invariant (auctionOpen = FALSE => bidCount > 0) derives from the business rule that an auction should only close after receiving at least one valid bid, preventing a vacuous auction. BidsBounded ensures both counters stay within Max. These invariants are sufficient because PlaceBid's guards prevent overflow, CloseAuction's guard ensures a non-empty auction, and no action can reopen the auction once closed, making the closed state terminal.

rationale: Good transaction lifecycle model with begin/op/commit/rollback. SafetyInv (idle => opCount=0) captures the key property that operation counts reset between transactions. Rollback correctly resets opCount. Missing concurrent transactions and isolation levels, but appropriate for the abstraction. Clean training example.
chain_of_thought: Database transactions require three state variables: `txState` (idle/active) to enforce that operations only occur within active transactions, `opCount` to track operations performed in the current transaction (reset on commit/rollback), and `committed` to count successfully committed transactions as a measure of system progress. The state space is bounded with txState in {idle, active}, opCount in 0..Max, and committed in 0..Max. Four actions model the transaction lifecycle: BeginTx transitions from idle to active, DoOp increments the operation count within an active transaction (guarded by opCount < Max), CommitTx ends the transaction successfully (resetting opCount to 0 and incrementing committed), and RollbackTx aborts without incrementing committed. The critical safety invariant (txState = idle => opCount = 0) derives from the requirement that operations only happen in active transactions: when a transaction ends (either commit or rollback), opCount must be reset. This invariant is sufficient because both CommitTx and RollbackTx set opCount' = 0 when transitioning to idle, and BeginTx does not modify opCount (which is already 0 in idle state by the invariant), so the property holds inductively.

rationale: Trivially a bounded counter. SafetyBounded is identical to TypeOK. No reorder points, no backorder tracking, no multiple SKUs. Only 4 states. This is the simplest possible spec and would not teach anything useful for inventory management or TLA+ beyond what a counter example shows.

Identical structure to inventory -- a single bounded counter. SafetyBounded is TypeOK restated. No participant identity, no messages, no ordering. Only 4 states. Structurally indistinguishable from WaterTank and Inventory specs. Would add no training value.

Adds a direction variable over a bare counter, but direction is never used in guards (can switch from moving up to moving down instantly). Idle action duplicates the UNCHANGED vars stutter. Only invariant is floor bounding. Missing requests, door state, and direction-based movement constraints.

Two independent bounded counters with no interaction. Commits and branches are completely decoupled -- creating a branch does not affect commits and vice versa. No merge, no checkout, no conflict. SafetyBounded just restates TypeOK. Teaches nothing about version control semantics.

Excellent spec. Correctly models task dependencies with guards (RunB requires a=2, RunC requires b=2). SafetyInvariant captures the core dependency constraint: b started implies a done, c started implies b done. Clean, non-trivial, and teaches dependency ordering patterns. Minor: hardcoded to 3 tasks, uses ints instead of named states.
chain_of_thought: The DAG dependency structure (A -> B -> C) requires three state variables `a`, `b`, `c` each encoding a task's lifecycle phase as an integer: 0=pending, 1=running, 2=done. This encoding is minimal because each task independently transitions through exactly three states, and the dependency is captured by guards on the predecessor's completion. The state space is 3^3 = 27 states, trivially bounded. Six actions model task execution: RunA/DoneA handle task A's lifecycle without preconditions, RunB requires a=2 (A must be done before B starts), DoneB completes B, RunC requires b=2 (B must be done before C starts), and DoneC completes C. The safety invariant (b > 0 => a = 2) /\ (c > 0 => b = 2) directly encodes the DAG dependency constraints: if B has started or finished, A must be done, and similarly for C depending on B. This invariant is sufficient because RunB's guard a=2 prevents B from starting before A completes, and RunC's guard b=2 prevents C from starting before B completes, so no execution order can violate the dependency chain.

rationale: Correct and clean with conservation invariant (avail + inuse = Max). However, this is structurally identical to ResourcePool and QuotaMgmt specs in this batch. Adding yet another conservation-pair spec provides diminishing training value. The spec itself is good but redundant.
chain_of_thought: The connection pool is a conservation system with fixed capacity, requiring two state variables `avail` and `inuse` to track the partition of connections between the free pool and active clients. The state space is bounded by Max for each variable, with the conservation invariant avail + inuse = Max further constraining reachable states to exactly Max+1 configurations along the diagonal. Borrow models a client acquiring a connection (guarded by avail > 0 to prevent over-allocation, atomically decrements avail and increments inuse), while Return models releasing it (guarded by inuse > 0, reverse transfer). The safety invariant SafetyConserved (avail + inuse = Max) derives from the physical requirement that connections cannot be created or destroyed, only moved between available and in-use pools. This invariant is inductively maintained because both Borrow and Return transfer exactly one unit between the two variables (+1 to one, -1 to the other), preserving the sum, and the stuttering step changes nothing.

rationale: Using integers 0-2 for health status is non-idiomatic (should use named states). The checks counter is bounded at 3 but this bound is never justified. Status and checks are completely independent -- probing does not affect status and vice versa. SafetyBounded just restates TypeOK. No actual health check logic.

Snapshot correctly resets logLen, which models compaction. But the snapshots counter is monotonically increasing with no purpose -- it is not used in any guard or invariant. SafetyBounded only bounds logLen. No event replay, no projection, no ordering. The interesting part of event sourcing (replay from log) is entirely absent.

Toggle only enables flags, Disable only disables -- no individual flag identity or toggle semantics. The 'total' variable is just a constant (never changes). SafetyBounded (enabled <= total) is trivially maintained. Structurally another bounded counter. No flag dependencies, no rollout percentages.

Two counters summing to at most Max. SafetyBounded is the only invariant and is straightforward. No reassignment between groups, no statistical significance tracking, no split ratio constraints. Structurally similar to many other specs in this batch. Too simple to provide meaningful training value.

Flush resets buffer to 0 regardless of batch size, losing information about how many items were in the batch. 'processed' counts flush operations, not processed items. SafetyBounded just restates TypeOK. No trigger condition (flush when full), no partial flush, no batch size tracking.

Good circuit breaker model with proper state transitions and threshold. SafetyInvariant is meaningful and non-trivial (open implies failures=3, half-open implies failures in {0,3}). Uses integers for states instead of strings which is less readable. Duplicate of manual2_circuit_breaker in concept, reducing marginal training value across the batch.
chain_of_thought: This circuit breaker uses integer encoding (0=closed, 1=open, 2=half-open) for the three states, plus a `failures` counter to track consecutive failures that trigger state transitions, requiring exactly two state variables. The state space is bounded: state in {0,1,2} and failures in 0..3, giving 12 possible configurations. Five actions model the breaker lifecycle: Fail increments the failure counter in closed state and transitions to open when failures reach 3, Succeed resets failures to 0 in closed state, Probe transitions from open to half-open (modeling a recovery attempt), ProbeOk resets to closed with zero failures on success, and ProbeFail returns to open on failure. The safety invariant (state = 1 => failures = 3) captures that the breaker only opens at the exact threshold, and (state = 2 => failures in {0, 3}) captures that half-open state is reached either directly from open (failures=3) or after a probe-ok reset (failures=0). These invariants are sufficient because Fail is the only transition to state=1 and it fires exactly when failures+1=3, Probe preserves the failure count, and ProbeOk resets it, covering all paths into each state.

rationale: Correct and clean with conservation invariant. However, structurally identical to ResourcePool, ConnPool, and CanaryDeploy. This is the fourth conservation-pair spec in these two batches. The spec is correct but provides zero marginal training value at this point.

Correct canary deployment model with conservation invariant (stable + canary = Max). Clean TLA+. But structurally identical to ResourcePool, ConnPool, and QuotaMgmt. No percentage thresholds, no health-based rollback triggers, no multi-stage rollout. Redundant as a training example.

Good model of mark-and-sweep GC phases. The phase-based state machine is correct and bounded. However, marking all objects is simplified to a counter increment loop rather than tracking individual object reachability, and the invariant SafetyBounded is largely redundant with TypeOK. Missing an invariant about phase consistency (e.g., marked=0 when phase=0). Still a useful training example for phase-based protocols.
chain_of_thought: The mark-and-sweep GC requires three state variables: `alloc` counting heap-allocated objects, `marked` counting objects identified as reachable during the mark phase, and `phase` (0=mutator, 1=marking, 2=sweeping) to enforce the sequential discipline of the GC algorithm. The state space is bounded by Max for the integer counters and {0,1,2} for phase, giving (Max+1)^2 * 3 states. Six actions model the GC lifecycle: AllocObj adds objects during the mutator phase, StartMark transitions to the mark phase (requires at least one allocated object), MarkObj increments the marked count up to alloc (modeling reachability analysis), StartSweep transitions to sweep phase, and Sweep atomically frees unmarked objects by setting alloc=marked and resetting marked=0 before returning to mutator phase. The safety invariant (marked <= alloc /\ alloc <= Max) ensures the mark count never exceeds allocation (you cannot mark more objects than exist) and the heap stays bounded. This is sufficient because MarkObj's guard (marked < alloc) prevents over-marking, Sweep reduces alloc to marked (preserving marked <= alloc trivially as alloc becomes marked and marked becomes 0), and AllocObj's guard prevents exceeding Max.

rationale: Correct but extremely trivial. SafetyInv is completely redundant with TypeOK since count is typed to 0..N. Only 4 distinct states. This teaches nothing beyond the absolute basics and the invariants add no insight. Too simple to be a valuable training example.

Correctly models alternating toggle with bounded count. However SafetyInv is redundant with TypeOK. Only 4 distinct states. The spec is too formulaic -- it is essentially a bounded counter with a string-valued state. No meaningful safety property beyond bounding. Does not teach anything a bounded counter does not already teach.

The MutualExclusion invariant is meaningful and well-stated. However, the model skips the 'waiting' state entirely despite mentioning it in the problem description -- processes jump from idle to critical. This means there is no actual contention modeled; it is a simplified check-and-enter. Not parameterized by process count. The invariant is good but the model is incomplete relative to the prompt.
chain_of_thought: Mutual exclusion between two processes requires two state variables `pc1` and `pc2` representing each process's program counter, because each process independently transitions through states and we need to express the global exclusion constraint. The state space is bounded at 3*3=9 configurations since each variable ranges over {idle, waiting, critical}. Four actions model process behavior: Enter1/Enter2 model a process attempting to enter the critical section (guarded by the other process not being in critical, encoding the mutual exclusion protocol), and Exit1/Exit2 model leaving the critical section. The safety invariant MutualExclusion (~(pc1 = "critical" /\ pc2 = "critical")) derives directly from the problem requirement that at most one process can be in the critical section. This invariant is sufficient because Enter1's guard (pc2 /= "critical") and Enter2's guard (pc1 /= "critical") prevent both processes from simultaneously entering critical, and since entry is the only action that sets a process to critical, the exclusion property is maintained inductively.

rationale: The spec models a single node entering/exiting CS and passing a token, but only tracks one node's CS status. With N nodes, each node should have its own state. The invariant TokenValid is redundant with TypeOK. Missing the key invariant that only the token-holding node can be in CS. The model does not capture the ring topology or per-node behavior. Incomplete and would teach a misleading pattern for token rings.

Correct producer-consumer model at an abstract level. However all four invariants (TypeOK, NoOverflow, NoUnderflow, SafetyInv) are logically equivalent or redundant -- bufLen in 0..BufSize already implies all three safety properties. Only 4 distinct states. The spec is just a bounded counter in disguise. Too trivial and the invariant redundancy would teach bad habits.

Good state-machine model with proper sequencing (Red->Green->Yellow->Red) and bounded cycles. The sequencing constraint is implicit in the action structure which is correct. CyclesBounded is somewhat redundant with TypeOK. Missing an explicit invariant about valid transitions (e.g., Green never directly follows Yellow). Decent training example for cyclic state machines.
chain_of_thought: The traffic light requires two state variables: `light` to track the current color (Red/Green/Yellow) and `cycles` to count completed full rotations, bounding the system to ensure finite model checking. The state space is 3 * (MaxCycles+1) states, bounded by the three light values and the MaxCycles constant. Three actions enforce the strict cyclic sequence: ToGreen fires only when light=Red (and cycles < MaxCycles to bound behavior), ToYellow fires only when light=Green, and ToRed fires only when light=Yellow and increments the cycle counter. The guard structure ensures the light follows Red->Green->Yellow->Red without skipping: each action's precondition restricts it to fire from exactly one color and transition to exactly the next color in the sequence. The safety invariant CyclesBounded (cycles <= MaxCycles) ensures the system terminates, and TypeOK ensures the light is always in a known state, which together are sufficient because the deterministic transition guards make it impossible to reach any color from the wrong predecessor.

rationale: Clean conservation-of-money invariant (acctA + acctB = TotalMoney) is the textbook example of a meaningful safety property. The invariant is non-trivial and genuinely constrains the system beyond type correctness. Model is simple but complete for the described problem. Transfers are unit-sized which limits realism but keeps it tractable. Strong training example for conservation invariants.
chain_of_thought: The bank transfer problem is a classic conservation system requiring two state variables `acctA` and `acctB` representing the balance in each account, because money moves between accounts and we must track both sides of every transfer. The state space is bounded by TotalMoney: each account ranges over 0..TotalMoney and the conservation invariant further constrains reachable states to TotalMoney+1 configurations along the diagonal acctA + acctB = TotalMoney. Two actions model the bidirectional transfer: TransferAtoB moves one unit from A to B (guarded by acctA > 0 to prevent overdraft), and TransferBtoA does the reverse (guarded by acctB > 0). The safety invariant MoneyConserved (acctA + acctB = TotalMoney) derives directly from the conservation-of-money requirement and is inductively maintained because each transfer atomically decrements one account and increments the other by the same amount. TypeOK (both accounts in 0..TotalMoney) combined with MoneyConserved is sufficient because overdraft prevention is enforced by the action guards, and the sum preservation ensures no money is created or lost.

rationale: The NoSimultaneousEating invariant is meaningful and correctly enforced by fork availability. However, the 'hungry' state from the problem description is missing. With only 2 philosophers sharing 2 forks, the model is correct but minimal. Not parameterized; hardcoded for 2 philosophers with individual variables instead of functions over a set. Fork states are derivable from philosopher states. Acceptable but not idiomatic for the classic problem.
chain_of_thought: The dining philosophers problem with 2 philosophers sharing 2 forks requires four state variables: `phil1` and `phil2` for each philosopher's state (thinking/eating), and `fork1` and `fork2` for each fork's availability (free/taken), because forks are shared resources whose state must be tracked independently to enforce the requirement that a philosopher needs both adjacent forks to eat. The state space is bounded at 2^2 * 2^2 = 16 configurations (2 philosopher states times 2 fork states each). Four actions model the system: Eat1/Eat2 model a philosopher picking up both forks and beginning to eat (guarded by both forks being free, ensuring atomic acquisition), and Done1/Done2 model finishing and releasing both forks. The safety invariant NoSimultaneousEating (~(phil1 = "eating" /\ phil2 = "eating")) follows from the shared fork constraint: since both philosophers need fork1 AND fork2, and Eat1/Eat2 both require fork1="free" /\ fork2="free", it is impossible for both to eat simultaneously because the first to eat sets both forks to "taken". This structural enforcement through fork guards makes the invariant inductively valid without requiring any additional synchronization mechanism.

rationale: Uses Sequences which is good, but the Enqueue action appends Len(queue)+1 as the value, meaning values are always sequential integers tied to queue length -- not a realistic or general queue. CapacityInv is redundant with TypeOK. The FIFO property is inherent in Sequences but never tested with a meaningful dequeue-value invariant. Would teach a questionable pattern for queue value generation.

AtMostOneLeader invariant is well-stated and meaningful. The model correctly enforces it via election guards. However, using individual variables (node1, node2, node3) instead of a function over a set is very non-idiomatic TLA+. Should use a VARIABLE nodeState with nodeState \in [Nodes -> {"follower", "leader"}]. The approach does not generalize and would teach bad habits for parameterized systems.
chain_of_thought: Leader election among 3 nodes requires three state variables `node1`, `node2`, `node3` each representing a node's role (follower/leader), because each node independently participates in election and we must express the global constraint that at most one is leader. The state space is bounded at 2^3 = 8 configurations. Six actions model the protocol: Elect1/Elect2/Elect3 each model a node becoming leader (guarded by the other two not being leaders, which enforces the single-leader constraint at election time), and Resign1/Resign2/Resign3 model a leader stepping down to follower. The safety invariant AtMostOneLeader is expressed as the conjunction of three pairwise exclusion clauses, which is equivalent to saying at most one node has value "leader". This invariant holds inductively because each ElectN action checks that the other two nodes are not leaders before promoting itself, and ResignN only reduces the number of leaders. The guard structure is sufficient because it prevents any election from creating a second leader, and resignation can only move the system closer to the zero-leader state.

rationale: Correct semaphore model but SemBounded is completely redundant with TypeOK. Only 4 distinct states. This is structurally identical to the bounded counter and bounded buffer specs -- just a counter that goes up and down within bounds. Does not model actual processes competing for the semaphore. Too simple and redundant with other specs in the batch.

Correct modeling of the described state transitions including the Error->Start recovery path. TransitionsBounded is redundant with TypeOK. Missing useful invariants like 'Done is a terminal state' or 'only Processing can reach Error'. The transitions counter adds boundedness but the invariant does not capture the interesting properties of the state machine. Decent structure but weak invariants.
chain_of_thought: The problem defines four discrete states with explicit transition edges, so the core variable is `state` drawn from {"Start", "Processing", "Done", "Error"}. To make model checking terminate we need a bounded transition counter `transitions` guarded by a CONSTANT MaxTransitions, which also prevents infinite cycling through Error->Start->Processing loops. Each action (StartToProcessing, ProcessingToDone, ProcessingToError, ErrorToStart) maps one-to-one to the allowed edges in the problem statement, with the guard `state = <source>` ensuring no illegal transitions. Note that "Done" is a terminal absorbing state since no outgoing edge is defined. TypeOK constrains state to the four-element set and transitions to 0..MaxTransitions, which together bound the state space to 4*(MaxTransitions+1) states. TransitionsBounded is the safety invariant ensuring the counter never exceeds its limit, and TypeOK's inclusion of state membership guarantees only legal state values are reachable.

rationale: Excellent spec. NoWriteWithReaders is a genuinely meaningful invariant that captures the core readers-writers safety property. The model correctly enforces mutual exclusion between writers and readers through action guards. Clean separation of read/write acquire/release. Would teach a strong pattern for shared-resource access control. One of the best specs in this batch.
chain_of_thought: The readers-writers problem requires tracking two orthogonal pieces of state: the count of concurrent readers (`readers` as a natural number) and whether a writer holds exclusive access (`writerActive` as a BOOLEAN). The state space is bounded by MaxReaders, giving (MaxReaders+1)*2 reachable states. AcquireRead models a reader entering the critical section, guarded by `writerActive = FALSE` to enforce mutual exclusion with writers and `readers < MaxReaders` to bound the count. AcquireWrite requires both `readers = 0` and `writerActive = FALSE`, enforcing the exclusive-access semantics that a writer cannot coexist with any reader or other writer. The key safety invariant NoWriteWithReaders (`writerActive = TRUE => readers = 0`) directly encodes the mutual exclusion requirement from the problem; it is sufficient because every action that sets writerActive to TRUE has an explicit guard readers = 0, and every action that increments readers has an explicit guard writerActive = FALSE.

rationale: Correct elevator movement with automatic direction reversal at boundaries. The direction logic in MoveUp and MoveDown is a nice touch. However FloorBounded is redundant with TypeOK. Missing invariants about direction consistency (e.g., if floor=0 then direction=up). No request queue or door logic. Acceptable but the invariants are weak.
chain_of_thought: The elevator's physical position requires variable `floor` bounded in 0..MaxFloor, and `direction` tracks intended travel to enable automatic reversal at boundaries. The state space is finite: (MaxFloor+1)*2 states. MoveUp models the elevator ascending one floor, guarded by `floor < MaxFloor` to enforce the upper bound, and automatically flips direction to "down" when hitting MaxFloor. MoveDown symmetrically guards `floor > 0` and flips to "up" at floor 0. The stuttering step (UNCHANGED) models the elevator idling. FloorBounded (`floor >= 0 /\ floor <= MaxFloor`) is the safety invariant derived directly from the constraint "must not go above MaxFloor or below 0"; it is sufficient because the only actions modifying floor are MoveUp and MoveDown, both of which have explicit preconditions preventing out-of-range transitions.

rationale: Correct turn alternation model. However MovesInBounds is redundant with TypeOK. Only 1 checked invariant beyond TypeOK. Only 4 distinct states. Missing invariants about turn consistency (e.g., if moves is even then turn=X). Too formulaic and trivial -- this is another bounded counter variant.

Good model of retry logic with pending/success/failed states and bounded attempts. GiveUp action correctly triggers on exhausted retries. However, despite the name 'RetryBackoff', there is no backoff modeled. AttemptsInBounds is redundant with TypeOK. Missing invariants like 'success is terminal' or 'failed implies max retries reached'. Decent structure, weak invariants.
chain_of_thought: The retry mechanism needs `attempts` to count how many tries have occurred and `status` to track the request lifecycle across {"pending", "success", "failed"}. The state space is bounded: (MaxRetries+1)*3 states, since attempts ranges over 0..MaxRetries and status has three values. TryAndSucceed models a successful attempt, TryAndFail models a failed attempt that remains pending for retry, and GiveUp models exhaustion of all retries transitioning to "failed" as a terminal outcome. The guard `attempts < MaxRetries` on both Try actions and `attempts >= MaxRetries` on GiveUp creates a clean partition ensuring the system either succeeds within the budget or gives up exactly at the boundary. AttemptsInBounds (`attempts <= MaxRetries`) is the key safety invariant; it is sufficient because every action that increments attempts is guarded by attempts < MaxRetries, so the counter can never exceed the bound.

rationale: Well-modeled 12-hour clock with correct hour wrapping (12->1) and minute overflow logic. 720 distinct states shows meaningful state space. The conditional logic in Tick is clean TLA+. HoursValid and MinutesValid are redundant with TypeOK, but TypeOK itself is well-defined. Would benefit from an invariant relating hours and minutes to a total elapsed count. Good training example for modular arithmetic patterns.
chain_of_thought: A 12-hour clock requires exactly two variables: `hours` in 1..12 and `minutes` in 0..59, giving a finite state space of 12*60 = 720 states. The single Tick action models the passage of one minute: minutes increments mod 60, and hours increments mod 12 (with the 12->1 wrap) only when minutes rolls over from 59 to 0. The conditional `IF minutes = 59` is the key branching logic -- it couples the minute rollover to the hour advance in a single atomic step, preventing any intermediate state where minutes = 0 but hours has not yet updated. TypeOK serves as the primary safety invariant constraining both variables to their valid domains. HoursValid and MinutesValid are equivalent restatements; they are sufficient because Tick is the only non-stuttering action and its arithmetic with the conditionals guarantees both variables stay within range.

rationale: This is literally the bounded counter spec with different variable names. Push=Increment, Pop=Decrement. DepthBounded is redundant with TypeOK. Does not actually model a stack (no values, no LIFO behavior, no Sequences). Would teach that a stack is just a counter, which is wrong. Should use Sequences to model actual stack operations.

Good multi-variable spec with meaningful StockConserved invariant (stock + dispensed = InitStock). Models insert, buy, and refund actions correctly. The conservation invariant is genuinely useful and non-trivial. Buy requires balance >= 2 which adds realistic pricing logic. BalanceBounded is somewhat redundant but StockConserved carries the spec. Solid training example.
chain_of_thought: The vending machine requires three variables: `balance` (coins inserted, bounded by MaxBalance), `stock` (remaining inventory), and `dispensed` (items given out) -- the latter two together track product conservation. The state space is bounded: (MaxBalance+1)*(InitStock+1)*(InitStock+1) states. InsertCoin models a customer adding money, Buy models a purchase requiring balance >= 2 and stock > 0 (atomically decrementing both and incrementing dispensed), and Refund models returning all coins. The conservation invariant StockConserved (`stock + dispensed = InitStock`) is the crucial safety property -- it is an exact invariant, not just a bound, because Buy is the only action modifying stock or dispensed and it decrements one while incrementing the other by exactly 1. BalanceBounded prevents coin overflow and is enforced by InsertCoin's guard `balance < MaxBalance`.

rationale: Good failure detection model. DeadMeansThreshold is a meaningful invariant that goes beyond type correctness -- it captures the relationship between status and missed count. The Recover action adds realistic behavior. MissHeartbeat correctly uses missedCount+1 >= Threshold for the transition. Clean and well-structured with 3 non-trivial invariants. Good training example for monitoring systems.
chain_of_thought: The heartbeat monitor needs `missedCount` to track consecutive missed heartbeats (resettable on receipt) and `nodeStatus` to record whether the node is considered alive or dead. The state space is bounded: (Threshold+1)*2 states. ReceiveHeartbeat resets the miss counter to 0, modeling a successful heartbeat arrival. MissHeartbeat increments the counter and transitions nodeStatus to "dead" exactly when missedCount+1 reaches Threshold, modeling a timeout event. Recover allows a dead node to return to alive with a reset counter, modeling a restart or reconnection. DeadMeansThreshold (`nodeStatus = "dead" => missedCount >= Threshold`) is the key safety invariant ensuring the system never falsely declares death; it holds because the only transition to "dead" occurs in MissHeartbeat when the post-increment value equals Threshold, and Recover resets both variables atomically.

rationale: The Reset action (decrementing consumed) makes no semantic sense for a pub/sub system -- consumed messages do not get unconsumed. This would teach a confusing pattern. SafetyBounded and SafetyValid are redundant with TypeOK. Missing conservation invariant (pending + consumed = total published). The model does not capture multiple topics or subscribers. Misleading Reset action lowers training value.

Clean model of block confirmation pipeline with bounded chain length. SafetyBounded (pending + confirmed <= Max) is a meaningful capacity invariant. However the model is quite abstract -- no hash chaining, no fork resolution, no consensus. SafetyValid is redundant with TypeOK. Acceptable as a simple bounded-resource spec but the blockchain framing is misleading for the level of abstraction.
chain_of_thought: The simplified blockchain model partitions blocks into two stages: `pending` (proposed but unconfirmed) and `confirmed` (finalized on chain), where their sum represents total chain length. Both variables are natural numbers bounded by Max, giving a state space of at most (Max+1)^2 states. AddBlock models a miner proposing a new block, guarded by `pending + confirmed < Max` to enforce the chain length bound. ConfirmBlock models finalization by atomically decrementing pending and incrementing confirmed, preserving the total. SafetyBounded (`pending + confirmed <= Max`) is the primary safety invariant derived from the bounded chain requirement; it holds because AddBlock is the only action increasing the sum and its guard prevents exceeding Max, while ConfirmBlock is sum-preserving.

rationale: The model has a subtle issue: checkedOut can grow independently of inCart since each Checkout only moves one item, but there is no conservation invariant. inCart and checkedOut are not constrained together. SafetyBounded and SafetyValid are redundant with TypeOK. Missing the key invariant that total items are conserved or bounded. The Checkout action is fine but the invariants miss the interesting property.

Good model with Enter, QueueUp, Admit, and Exit covering the full lifecycle. The guard on Enter (waiting=0) ensuring direct entry only when no queue is a nice detail. Admit correctly handles queue-to-lot transitions. However SafetyBounded and SafetyValid are redundant with TypeOK. Missing an invariant about queue fairness or that occupied <= Max is maintained when Admit fires. Structure is good but invariants are weak.
chain_of_thought: The parking lot requires `occupied` to track cars in spaces and `waiting` for the overflow queue, both bounded by Max. The state space is (Max+1)^2. Enter models a vehicle driving in when spaces are free and no queue exists (the `waiting = 0` guard ensures FIFO fairness -- a direct arrival cannot bypass queued vehicles). QueueUp models overflow when the lot is full. Admit models dequeuing a waiting vehicle into a freed space, atomically decrementing waiting and incrementing occupied. Exit models a vehicle leaving. SafetyBounded (`occupied <= Max /\ waiting <= Max`) is the key invariant; it is sufficient because Enter and Admit both guard `occupied < Max` before incrementing, and QueueUp guards `waiting < Max`.

rationale: Excellent spec. SafetyValid (engaged=1 => speed>0) is a genuinely meaningful invariant that captures a real safety property -- cruise control must disengage at zero speed. BrakeStop correctly auto-disengages at speed=1->0. The separation of Brake and BrakeStop handles the boundary case cleanly. SafetyBounded is redundant but SafetyValid makes up for it. Strong training example for safety-critical systems.
chain_of_thought: Cruise control requires `speed` (0..Max) for the vehicle's current velocity and `engaged` (0 or 1) for the cruise control state, giving a state space of (Max+1)*2. Engage requires `speed > 0` because cruise control is undefined at standstill, directly yielding the safety invariant SafetyValid (`engaged = 1 => speed > 0`). The critical modeling insight is splitting braking into two actions: Brake (speed > 1, normal deceleration) and BrakeStop (speed = 1, which atomically reduces speed to 0 AND disengages cruise control). This two-action decomposition prevents a state where engaged = 1 and speed = 0, which would violate SafetyValid. Accelerate and Brake are guarded by their respective bounds (speed < Max, speed > 1) ensuring SafetyBounded. The invariants are sufficient because every action that could create an engaged-at-zero state (BrakeStop) explicitly sets engaged to 0.

rationale: Strong spec of 2PC protocol. SafetyInv (committed => both yes) is the textbook safety property and is genuinely non-trivial. The protocol flow (init->waiting->committed/aborted) with voting is correctly modeled. DecideAbort properly handles all non-unanimous cases. Verbose UNCHANGED clauses reduce idiom score slightly. 14 distinct states show good state space. Excellent training example for distributed protocols.
chain_of_thought: Two-phase commit requires `coordState` to track the coordinator's protocol phase (init/waiting/committed/aborted) and per-participant vote variables `p1Vote` and `p2Vote` each in {"none", "yes", "no"}, giving a finite state space of 4*3*3 = 36 states. Prepare models the coordinator broadcasting the prepare message, transitioning from init to waiting. The four vote actions (P1VoteYes, P1VoteNo, P2VoteYes, P2VoteNo) model each participant's autonomous decision, guarded by the vote being "none" to ensure each participant votes exactly once. DecideCommit requires both votes to be "yes", while DecideAbort fires when both have voted and at least one said "no". The safety invariant `coordState = "committed" => (p1Vote = "yes" /\ p2Vote = "yes")` directly encodes the atomic commit requirement that unanimous agreement is necessary for commit; it is sufficient because DecideCommit is the only action setting coordState to "committed" and its guard explicitly checks both votes are "yes".

rationale: Well-designed permission model. NoWriteWithoutRead (write => read) captures a real dependency constraint. RevokeRead correctly guards against revoking read when write is active. The access counter adds depth. 24 distinct states with 3 meaningful invariants. Clean separation of grant/revoke actions. Would teach good patterns for permission and dependency modeling.
chain_of_thought: The file permission system requires three BOOLEAN variables (canRead, canWrite, canExec) for the permission bits plus `accessCount` bounded by Max to track access attempts, giving a state space of 2^3 * (Max+1). The key dependency constraint is that write requires read, which manifests in two places: GrantWrite guards `canRead = TRUE`, and RevokeRead guards `canWrite = FALSE` to prevent revoking read while write is still active. This bidirectional enforcement is necessary because either granting write without read or revoking read while write persists would violate NoWriteWithoutRead. The Access action models any file operation, requiring at least one permission bit to be true and incrementing the bounded counter. NoWriteWithoutRead (`canWrite = TRUE => canRead = TRUE`) is the structural safety invariant encoding the permission dependency; SafetyInv (`accessCount <= Max`) bounds the access counter. Together they are sufficient because every action that modifies permissions respects the dependency guards and Access is the only action incrementing the counter with an explicit bound check.

rationale: Excellent spec. SafetyInv (accepted => majority yes) is a strong safety property. VotesBounded is meaningful given yes and no are tracked separately. The majority check (yesVotes*2 > N) correctly handles both even and odd N. DecideReject uses negation of majority which is correct. 14 distinct states with 3 invariants. Teaches consensus, majority voting, and decision finality. Top-tier training example.
chain_of_thought: The consensus protocol requires `yesVotes` and `noVotes` as separate counters (rather than a single total) because the decision rule is majority-based and needs to compare yes votes against the total N. The `decided` variable in {"pending", "accepted", "rejected"} tracks whether a decision has been reached, preventing further voting or re-decision. The state space is bounded: (N+1)^2 * 3 states, since yesVotes and noVotes each range over 0..N. VoteYes and VoteNo model individual ballot casting, guarded by `decided = "pending" /\ yesVotes + noVotes < N` to ensure votes only occur before the tally is complete. DecideAccept and DecideReject both require all N votes to be in (`yesVotes + noVotes = N`), with DecideAccept additionally requiring strict majority (`yesVotes * 2 > N`) -- using multiplication instead of division avoids integer rounding issues. The safety invariant `decided = "accepted" => yesVotes * 2 > N` ensures acceptance only with true majority; it is sufficient because DecideAccept is the sole action setting decided to "accepted" and its guard enforces the majority condition.

rationale: Hardcodes N==5 instead of using CONSTANT, defeating parameterization. Fork model uses integer ownership which is reasonable but LEFT/RIGHT indexing for fork pickup vs putdown is inconsistent (picks up fork[i] and fork[LEFT(i)] but puts down fork[i] and fork[RIGHT(i)], which is actually correct for left=own, right=neighbor). Only TypeOK invariant -- missing mutual exclusion (no two adjacent philosophers eating), which is the whole point of dining philosophers. EXTENDS FiniteSets but never uses it.

Uses CONSTANT N and BOOLEAN forks which is cleaner. However, the 'becomes hungry' action has no guard on current state -- a philosopher who is already eating or hungry can become hungry again, which is a correctness bug. Only TypeOK invariant: completely missing mutual exclusion (adjacent philosophers cannot eat simultaneously), the core safety property. Only 32 distinct states with presumably small N suggests limited exploration.

Fundamentally broken: ProducerAction assigns q' twice (once via EXCEPT, once via set membership constraint), which is either unsatisfiable or confusing. Same issue in ConsumerAction. Uses array-based circular buffer but also has redundant q' constraint. Only TypeOK invariant with no queue safety properties. Would teach terrible habits.

Array-based circular buffer is a valid approach. ConsumerAction does not check q[head]=1 before consuming (unlike ProducerAction which checks q[tail]=0), but size guard may suffice if invariants hold. TypeOK has a redundant last conjunct (q[i] in {0,1} is implied by q in [1..K -> {0,1}]). Only TypeOK invariant -- no FIFO ordering or size consistency property. Redundant EXTENDS FiniteSets/Sequences.

Clean array-based circular buffer, producer/consumer logic is correct. But only TypeOK invariant -- no safety properties about queue ordering, size consistency, or data integrity. Does not actually model FIFO ordering (slots are just 0/1 flags). Would not teach a student what invariants matter for a bounded queue.

Confused design: uses TLA+ Seq (Append/Tail) for the queue which inherently provides FIFO, but also maintains head/tail circular buffer pointers that are completely redundant and never used for actual access. TypeOK claims q in Seq(1..K) but elements are always literal 1. Only TypeOK invariant. The redundant head/tail variables teach a confused mental model mixing two different queue implementations.

Identical to benchmark_56 -- same Seq-based queue with redundant head/tail circular buffer pointers. Same confused design mixing two queue implementations. Only TypeOK invariant. No training value as a near-duplicate.

Parameterized Enqueue(e) with e in 1..K is slightly better than hardcoded value 1, giving more state space (120 states). Still has redundant head/tail pointers alongside Seq operations. Only TypeOK invariant -- no FIFO ordering or size==Len(q) invariant. Marginally better than the dummy-value variants but still teaches the confused Seq+circular-buffer anti-pattern.
Virtually identical to benchmark_58. Parameterized Enqueue(e) with Seq operations plus redundant head/tail. Only TypeOK invariant. Another near-duplicate with no additional training value.

Uses UNCHANGED which is slightly more idiomatic than explicit head'=head. Still has the Seq+head/tail redundancy. Appends dummy value 1. Only TypeOK. One minor positive: comments clearly label 'blocks when full/empty' but the spec does not actually model blocking -- it simply has no enabled action, which is stuttering not blocking.

Has a subtle operator precedence bug: 'Next == \E e \in 1..K : Enqueue(e) \/ Dequeue' -- the existential quantifier binds over the entire disjunction, meaning Dequeue is guarded by the existential, which is semantically wrong (Dequeue does not use e). This would confuse learners about TLA+ operator precedence. Redundant head/tail with Seq. Only TypeOK.

Same operator precedence issue as benchmark_61: existential quantifier scopes over both Enqueue(x) and Dequeue. Dequeue doesn't use x so it's not incorrect per se (just evaluates Dequeue K times redundantly), but it teaches bad style. Near-duplicate of 61. Redundant head/tail. Only TypeOK.

Another duplicate of the Enqueue(e)/Dequeue pattern with the same existential scoping issue. Redundant head/tail alongside Seq. Only TypeOK invariant. No additional training value over benchmarks 61/62.

Clean naming (Produce/Consume), uses UNCHANGED properly. But same fundamental issues: Seq+head/tail redundancy, dummy enqueue value, only TypeOK invariant. Module name BoundedFIFO is slightly better. Near-duplicate of many others in this batch.

Yet another Seq-based bounded FIFO with redundant head/tail and dummy placeholder value. Only TypeOK invariant. Indistinguishable in quality from benchmarks 56, 57, 60, 64, 68, 72, 75. The massive duplication of this pattern across the dataset is itself a training data quality problem.

ProducerAction appends 'head' to queue (queue' = Append(queue, head)), which makes no semantic sense -- the head pointer is not a data element. Uses modular arithmetic for circular indexing (tail % K + 1) which is fine. TypeOK includes K in Nat which is a constant check, not a state invariant. Only TypeOK. Confused semantics.
Same Seq+head/tail pattern seen in benchmarks 58-63. Parameterized Enqueue(e) provides 120 states. Same existential scoping issue with Next. Only TypeOK. Yet another near-duplicate.
Clean structure with separate Enqueue/Dequeue operators, but same Seq+head/tail redundancy. Dummy value 1. Only TypeOK invariant. Duplicate of benchmarks 65, 72, 75.

Pure array-based circular buffer without Seq -- at least internally consistent. Uses EXCEPT for slot updates. ProducerAction marks q[head]=1 but should mark q[tail]=1 (writes to head instead of tail position), which is a correctness bug. Only TypeOK invariant. Slightly better than the Seq+head/tail hybrids but the head/tail confusion is a real error.
Inline producer/consumer in Next is acceptable style. Same Seq+head/tail redundancy. Dummy value. Only TypeOK. Module named BoundedFIFO. Another duplicate.

Pure array-based circular buffer, internally consistent. ProducerAction writes to queue[tail] and ConsumerAction reads from queue[head], which is correct circular buffer semantics. TypeOK uses [1..K -> 0..1] which is appropriate. Best of the array-based variants. But still only TypeOK invariant -- missing count==number of 1s in queue, or FIFO ordering. Borderline keep as the single best representative of the array-based approach.
Another Seq+head/tail duplicate. Identical pattern to benchmarks 65, 68, 75. Dummy value. Only TypeOK. No training value given the massive duplication.

Uses CONSTANTS (plural) for single constant K which is technically valid but non-standard. TypeOK includes K in Nat which checks the constant, not state. Inline Next with dummy Append. Seq+head/tail redundancy. Another duplicate with no distinguishing quality.

head and tail are declared as variables but NEVER modified in Next (both actions set head'=head, tail'=tail). They are completely dead variables initialized to 0 and never used. Only 4 distinct states confirms trivial state space. TypeOK allows head/tail in 0..K but they are always 0. Uses Len(q) for bounds which is fine, but the dead variables are a serious quality issue. Would teach terrible habits.
Exact duplicate of benchmarks 65, 68, 72. Seq+head/tail, dummy value, only TypeOK. Zero marginal training value.

Fundamentally broken logic: producer acts when head != tail (which in a circular buffer means NOT empty, not 'not full'), and consumer acts when head == tail (which means empty, the opposite of when consuming should be possible). The empty/full conditions are inverted. No size variable to track occupancy. Array-based but with wrong semantics. Only TypeOK with overly broad Nat range. Would actively teach incorrect queue logic.

Best idiomatic style in the batch: uses vars tuple, UNCHANGED with angle brackets, clean operator naming. But same Seq+head/tail redundancy and dummy value. Only TypeOK. The good style is undermined by the confused design. Marginally better than most duplicates due to idiom quality.
Yet another Seq+head/tail duplicate with dummy value and only TypeOK. Uses explicit head'=head instead of UNCHANGED. Indistinguishable from many others. No training value.

Same Append(q, head) semantic confusion as benchmark_66 -- appends the head pointer as a data element. Inline Next without named operators. Modular arithmetic indexing. TypeOK includes K in Nat. Indentation is inconsistent (mixed alignment in Next). Another near-duplicate with a semantic bug.

Fundamentally broken Raft election: currentTerm is a single shared variable instead of per-server (should be [1..N -> Nat]). Candidate and Leader actions are pure no-ops (stuttering steps). There is no voting mechanism -- a follower becomes a candidate and votes for itself, but no other server ever grants votes. No majority check, no leader transition. The spec models nothing meaningful about Raft leader election. 8 distinct states confirms trivial exploration. Would actively teach a wrong model of Raft.

Token ring that only tracks token position with no messaging modeled despite the prompt requiring it. Init nondeterministically picks token position which is fine but the Next has a redundant conjunct (token' \in 1..N is implied by the IF). Only invariant is TypeOK which is trivial. No safety property about 'only token holder may send'. Would teach students that TypeOK-only specs are acceptable.

Uses %(token, N) which is not valid TLA+ syntax (modular arithmetic is not a built-in operator). Models messages accumulating per node which is a nice touch, but the broken modulo operator means this spec will not parse in TLC. Only TypeOK as invariant, no safety property about exclusive token-based sending. Msg as a constant with messages being sets of {Msg} is odd.

The Next action uses \E i \in 1..N to pick any i and then sets token' to i+1 (mod N), but does not constrain that i = token. This means the token can jump to any position nondeterministically, completely breaking the clockwise passing semantics. Only TypeOK invariant. Fundamentally incorrect model that would teach wrong patterns.

Uses %(token, N) which is not valid TLA+ syntax. The intent (modular increment) is correct but the spec will not parse. No messaging modeled, no meaningful invariant beyond TypeOK. Too minimal to be a useful training example even if the syntax were fixed.

Identical issues to gold_all_benchmarks_124: invalid %(token, N) syntax, no messaging, TypeOK-only invariant. This is essentially a duplicate of 124 with trivially different formatting. Would pollute training data with broken syntax.

Identical to 125 and 124. Invalid % operator, no messages, trivial invariant. Another near-duplicate that adds no value. Including multiple nearly-identical broken specs in training data would reinforce bad patterns.

Token passing logic is correct (IF token=N THEN 1 ELSE token+1) but has redundant conjunct token' \in 1..N. Extends TLC module unnecessarily. Init is nondeterministic which is acceptable. But no messaging, only TypeOK invariant, and no meaningful safety property. Too trivial for training.

Same fundamental bug as 123: \E i \in 1..N picks any node i and sets token to i+1, ignoring current token position. Token can jump anywhere. This is not a correct token ring. TypeOK-only invariant. Would actively teach incorrect modeling.
