Correct clockwise token passing with IF/THEN/ELSE. Cleaner than 127 but still has redundant token' \in 1..N conjunct. Extends only Naturals and FiniteSets which is more appropriate. Still no messaging and only TypeOK. Marginally better idiom but too trivial and near-duplicate of many others.

Cleanest of the token ring variants: deterministic Init (token=1), correct Next, proper vars tuple. But still only models token position with TypeOK invariant, missing the messaging aspect of the prompt. The problem asks for 'only token holder may send' but there is no send action or invariant enforcing this. Slightly better than siblings but still incomplete.

Correct token passing, nondeterministic init. Uses <<token>> inline in Spec which is fine. Extends Sequences unnecessarily. Only TypeOK invariant, no messaging. Yet another near-duplicate of the same minimal token ring pattern. Having this many near-duplicates in training data is harmful.
Invalid %(token, N) syntax again. Deterministic Init (token=1). Only TypeOK. Another duplicate of the broken modulo pattern. No new training signal.

Claims to be Peterson's algorithm but is fundamentally wrong. Peterson's requires a pc (program counter) tracking process phases (idle, wanting, critical). This spec only has flag and turn with flat disjunctive actions that do not model the algorithm's phases. No mutual exclusion invariant is stated. The actions allow both flags to be set simultaneously with no exclusion check. Does not model Peterson's algorithm at all, just arbitrary flag toggling.

Claims to be Dekker's algorithm but is completely wrong. There is no program counter tracking process state. The transitions do not model Dekker's protocol at all - they just toggle wants flags based on current state. No critical section, no mutual exclusion invariant. Only 2 distinct states indicates a degenerate model. The prompt asks for both safety and liveness but neither is modeled. Would teach fundamentally incorrect understanding of Dekker's algorithm.

Nearly identical to 192 with same fundamental problems. Not Dekker's algorithm, no pc variable, no critical section concept, no mutual exclusion invariant. The difference from 192 is minor (last disjunct keeps wants unchanged vs clearing). Still only 2 distinct states. Completely unsuitable as training data for Dekker's algorithm.

Worst of the Dekker variants. Init has redundant conjuncts (turn \in {0,1} stated twice, wants constrained twice). TypeOK also has redundant conjuncts. Uses wants as a SET rather than a function which is non-standard for this algorithm. No pc, no critical section, no mutual exclusion. Actions are arbitrary set operations with no relationship to Dekker's protocol. Would teach terrible TLA+ habits.

Slightly better than other Dekker variants: uses function for wants, has turn toggling and flag setting. But Init has redundant conjunct (wants \in [0..1 -> BOOLEAN] then wants = [...|-> FALSE]). TypeOK has redundant third conjunct. Still no pc, no critical section, no mutual exclusion invariant. Only 4 distinct states. Does not model Dekker's algorithm.

Another broken Dekker variant with redundant Init conjuncts, set-based wants, and guard conditions in Next that are redundant with variable types. No pc, no critical section, no mutual exclusion. The second disjunct has turn \in {0,1} and wants \subseteq {0,1} as guards in Next which are always true - shows fundamental misunderstanding. Completely unsuitable.

Clean, idiomatic mutual exclusion spec with proper three-phase protocol (idle/trying/critical). MutualExclusion invariant correctly captures the key safety property. TypeOK is well-defined. Uses ASSUME for N, proper operator decomposition. Minor gap: no fairness or liveness property, and the Enter guard silently assumes strongest possible exclusion check. Good training example that teaches clean structure.
chain_of_thought: The mutual exclusion problem for N processes requires a single state variable pc mapping each process in Procs (1..N) to one of three program counter values: idle, trying, or critical. A single function suffices because each process's behavior is fully characterized by its phase in the protocol. The ASSUME N in 1..10 bounds the constant for TLC model checking. Three named actions decompose the protocol: TryEnter(p) models a process requesting the critical section; Enter(p) models actual entry, guarded by the universal quantifier ensuring no other process is critical; Exit(p) models release back to idle. The MutualExclusion invariant directly encodes the safety requirement: for any two distinct processes, they cannot both be in critical simultaneously. This invariant is sufficient because it is exactly the negation of the unsafe state, and it is preserved by Next because Enter's guard explicitly checks for the absence of any other critical process before allowing entry.

rationale: Excellent two-phase commit spec with well-decomposed actions for each protocol phase. Models coordinator states, participant states, voting, and decision propagation correctly. Consistency invariant captures that committed participants imply commit decision. Minor: AbortPart allows aborting 'working' participants which is debatable, and the Consistency invariant could be stronger (e.g., no committed+aborted mix). Very high training value due to realistic distributed systems pattern.
chain_of_thought: Two-phase commit requires three state variables: coordState tracking the coordinator's phase (init/waiting/committed/aborted), partState mapping each participant to its local state (working/prepared/aborted/committed), and decision recording the global outcome (none/commit/abort). Three variables are necessary because the coordinator's state, each participant's state, and the commit decision evolve semi-independently -- participants vote asynchronously, and the decision is a separate global fact. The protocol phases map to actions: Prepare moves the coordinator from init to waiting; VoteYes/VoteNo model each participant's response; DecideCommit fires only when all participants are prepared (the unanimity requirement); DecideAbort fires when any participant aborted. CommitPart and AbortPart propagate the decision to individual participants. The Consistency invariant states that any committed participant implies decision = commit, which is sufficient because DecideCommit only fires when all votes are prepared, and CommitPart only fires when decision = commit, so no participant can reach committed unless the unanimous vote occurred.

rationale: Solid Bakery algorithm implementation with doorway, waiting, and critical phases. MutualExclusion invariant is correct. The Max operator using CHOOSE is idiomatic. The bound on num (0..20) with guard Max < 18 prevents overflow, though this is an artificial bound. The two-step doorway (set flag then clear) is a reasonable abstraction. 1927 distinct states indicates meaningful state exploration. Minor: flag semantics differ slightly from Lamport's original but the safety property holds.
chain_of_thought: Lamport's bakery algorithm requires three state variables: num (ticket numbers), flag (doorway flags), and pc (program counters). The num variable maps each process to its drawn ticket number (0 means inactive); flag indicates whether a process is currently in the doorway (choosing a number); pc tracks the protocol phase (idle/doorway/waiting/critical). The Doorway action draws a ticket by computing Max of all current tickets plus one, and sets the flag to TRUE to signal the doorway is being traversed. FinishDoorway clears the flag. EnterCS is guarded by two conditions for each other process: the other's flag is FALSE (not mid-doorway) and either the other has no ticket or the current process has a strictly smaller ticket (with process ID as tiebreaker). The bound Max < 18 on ticket values ensures finite state space for TLC (20^N * 2^N * 4^N states). The MutualExclusion invariant holds because two processes in critical would require each to have passed the other's doorway check, but the ticket ordering with tiebreaker is a total order, making simultaneous passage impossible.

rationale: Reasonable key-value store model but the Linearizability invariant is weak. It only checks that lastGet[k] equals store[k] or is 0, which is always true by construction since Get reads directly from store. A true linearizability property would need concurrent operations and ordering. The spec is really just a sequential store with a read cache. Decent TLA+ idiom but the invariant gives false confidence about linearizability.
chain_of_thought: A key-value store needs two state variables: store (the actual map from keys to values) and lastGet (recording the most recent Get result per key). The store variable is necessary to track the current database state; lastGet is necessary to express the linearizability property as a state predicate rather than a trace property. Both map Keys (1..N) to Values (1..N) union {0}, where 0 represents the initial empty/unset state. Put(k,v) updates store[k] to v; Get(k) copies store[k] into lastGet[k]. The state space is bounded because both keys and values come from finite sets 1..N plus the sentinel 0, giving (N+1)^(2N) states. The Linearizability invariant asserts that for every key, lastGet[k] equals either store[k] or 0 (the initial default). This is sufficient because Get atomically reads the current store value, so lastGet can never hold a stale non-zero value that disagrees with store -- it either reflects the latest Put or has never been read.

rationale: Ambitious Paxos spec with quorums, ballots, prepare/accept/choose phases. Structure is reasonable. However, the Consistency invariant is too weak - it only checks chosen is in Values, not that once chosen it never changes (which is guaranteed by the guard chosen=-1 in Choose but should be stated). The Accept phase's value selection logic (promisedVals check) is simplified - it should pick the value associated with the highest maxVBal, not just any value in promisedVals. This could allow inconsistency with multiple proposers.
chain_of_thought: Single-decree Paxos requires four state variables: maxBal (highest ballot each acceptor has promised), maxVBal (highest ballot at which each acceptor accepted a value), maxVal (the value accepted at that ballot), and chosen (the globally chosen value, -1 if none). These four are necessary because Paxos separates the promise phase (tracked by maxBal) from the accept phase (tracked by maxVBal/maxVal), and the chosen value is a derived global fact. Quorum is defined as any subset of acceptors with more than half the population, ensuring any two quorums overlap. Prepare(b) models Phase 1a/1b: a proposer gets a quorum to promise ballot b by advancing their maxBal. Accept(b,v) models Phase 2a/2b: given a quorum that promised b, the proposer picks v consistent with the highest previously accepted value (or freely if none), and the quorum accepts. Choose models learning: when a quorum has accepted the same value, it becomes chosen. The Consistency invariant (chosen in Values when set) is maintained because quorum overlap ensures any two Accept actions on different ballots must agree on the value, preventing divergence.

rationale: Excellent bounded retransmission spec. Models the full protocol: send, lose data, deliver, lose ack, receive ack, timeout with retry limit. Channel states are well-chosen. DeliveryOrFailure invariant correctly captures the key safety property. State decomposition into clear named actions is exemplary. The Done action properly stutters. Very high training value - teaches realistic protocol modeling with failure handling.
chain_of_thought: The bounded retransmission protocol requires six state variables: sChunk (sender's current chunk index), rChunk (receiver's last received chunk), retry (current retry count), sDone/rDone (sender/receiver completion status), and channel (modeling the unreliable medium). Six variables are needed because the protocol tracks sender progress, receiver progress, retry budget, both sides' completion status, and the channel state independently. The channel is modeled as a single-message buffer with five states: empty, data, lost, ack, ack_lost. Send places data on the channel; Deliver moves data to receiver and produces an ack; Lose and LoseAck model message loss; RecvAck advances the sender to the next chunk; Timeout handles lost messages by either retrying (incrementing retry up to MAX) or declaring failure. The state space is bounded by N chunks, MAX retries, and the finite channel states. The DeliveryOrFailure invariant states that sDone=ok implies all chunks were received (rChunk=N), which holds because RecvAck only sets sDone=ok when sChunk=N and a valid ack was received, meaning the last chunk was successfully delivered.

rationale: Well-structured snapshot isolation spec with proper transaction lifecycle (begin, write, commit with conflict check, abort). The write-write conflict detection on commit is correct. NoWriteConflict invariant properly captures the safety property. The commit action's conflict check against already-committed transactions is the right approach. Minor: committed values are snapshot+1 rather than explicit write values, but this works for the abstraction. 37899 states indicates thorough exploration. Excellent training example for database concurrency.
chain_of_thought: Snapshot isolation requires four state variables: db (the committed database state), txState (each transaction's lifecycle phase), txSnapshot (the database snapshot each transaction reads from), and txWrites (the set of keys each transaction has written). Four variables are needed because SI decouples read visibility (snapshot) from write tracking (write set), and both are per-transaction. Begin(t) captures the current db into txSnapshot[t], giving the transaction a consistent read view. Write(t,k) adds k to the write set without modifying db -- writes are buffered. Commit(t) checks that no already-committed transaction has a conflicting write set (the write-write conflict detection that defines SI), then applies buffered writes to db. Abort(t) simply marks the transaction as aborted without modifying db. The state space is bounded: db has values in 0..5 per key, txState has 4 phases per transaction, txWrites is a subset of N keys. The NoWriteConflict invariant -- no two committed transactions share written keys -- is sufficient because the Commit guard explicitly checks for disjoint write sets against all previously committed transactions before allowing the commit to proceed.

rationale: Clock synchronization model with averaging approach. The Sync action correctly picks a peer and averages, tracking which nodes have synced. However, ClockBound with range -5..5 is extremely weak for an 'epsilon' bound - with initial clocks in 0..3, integer division averaging should bring them much closer. The invariant is essentially always true and would not catch bugs. Also, each node syncs exactly once with one arbitrary peer, which is a very simplified model of clock sync. Decent structure but weak invariant.
chain_of_thought: Clock synchronization requires two state variables: clock (mapping each node to its current clock value) and synced (tracking whether each node has completed its synchronization round). The clock variable is initialized nondeterministically from [Nodes -> 0..3] to model initial clock drift. The synced variable is necessary to enforce a single-round protocol: each node synchronizes exactly once. The Sync(i) action picks an arbitrary other node j, computes the integer average of their clocks, and updates node i's clock to that average, then marks i as synced. Integer division introduces rounding but preserves boundedness within 0..10. The Done action is a stuttering step for when all nodes have synced. The state space is bounded because clocks range over 0..10 and synced is Boolean per node. The ClockBound invariant asserts that after all nodes have synced, any two clocks differ by at most 5, which is sufficient because pairwise averaging with initial spread of at most 3 cannot diverge clocks beyond the bounded range, and the -5..5 bound is conservative for this initial condition.

rationale: Clean memory allocator spec with proper page pool management. SafeAllocation invariant correctly ensures no page is double-allocated. The free/allocated partition is well-modeled. Allocate and Release actions are clean with proper set operations. Minor: no conservation invariant (free \cup all allocated = Pages) which would strengthen the spec. Good training value for resource management patterns.
chain_of_thought: A memory allocator needs two state variables: free (the set of currently unallocated pages) and allocated (a function mapping each client to the set of pages it holds). Two variables are needed because the allocator must track both the pool of available pages and per-client ownership. Init sets free = Pages (all pages available) and allocated to empty sets for all clients. Allocate(c) picks a page from the free pool, removes it from free, and adds it to allocated[c] -- modeling a client requesting a page. Release(c) picks a page from allocated[c], removes it, and returns it to free -- modeling a client freeing a page. The state space is bounded because Pages is finite (1..N) and each page is either free or allocated to exactly one client, giving at most (N+1)^N configurations. The SafeAllocation invariant asserts that for any two distinct clients, their allocated sets are disjoint, which is sufficient because Allocate only draws from free (which is disjoint from all allocated sets by construction), so a page can never appear in two clients' sets simultaneously.

rationale: Pub-sub broker with subscriptions, publishing, and delivery. Structure is clean. However, DeliveryGuarantee only states delivered <= published, which is trivially maintained by construction (Deliver increments only when delivered < published). A meaningful invariant would be something about eventual delivery or no message loss. The MaxMsgs=1 constant severely limits the model. The Done action does not require all deliveries to complete. Decent pattern but weak invariant.
chain_of_thought: The pub-sub broker requires three state variables: subscriptions (mapping each subscriber to its set of subscribed topics), published (counting messages published per topic), and delivered (counting per-subscriber-per-topic deliveries). Three variables are needed because subscription registration, message production, and delivery progress are independent concerns. Subscribe(s,t) adds topic t to subscriber s's subscription set. Publish(t) increments the published count for topic t, bounded by MaxMsgs to keep the state space finite. Deliver(s,t) increments delivered[s][t] only if t is in s's subscriptions and delivered count is below published count -- modeling the broker forwarding a message. The state space is bounded by the finite topic/subscriber sets and the MaxMsgs cap on counters. The DeliveryGuarantee invariant states that for any subscriber s subscribed to topic t, delivered[s][t] <= published[t], which is sufficient because Deliver's guard explicitly requires delivered < published before incrementing, so over-delivery is impossible, and the invariant captures that no subscriber receives more messages than were actually published.

rationale: The best spec in both batches. Proper Dekker's algorithm with full pc-based state machine (idle, set_flag, check, wait, critical, exit). Correctly models the flag-setting, checking, backing-off, and turn-based arbitration of the real algorithm. MutualExclusion invariant is correct and meaningful. Deadlock_Freedom property is a nice bonus. Clean operator decomposition with Other(p) helper. 40 distinct states is appropriate for 2-process Dekker. Excellent training example.
chain_of_thought: Dekker's algorithm for 2 processes requires three state variables: wants (Boolean flags indicating intent to enter), turn (the tiebreaker variable alternating between processes), and pc (a 6-state program counter per process tracking protocol phases). The wants flags are necessary for the flag-based signaling that distinguishes Dekker from simpler lock algorithms. The turn variable resolves contention when both processes want entry simultaneously. The pc tracks: idle (not competing), set_flag (flag raised, about to check), check (inspecting the other's flag), wait (backing off when contention detected and it's not our turn), critical, and exit. The key Dekker logic is in Wait(p): if the other process's flag is raised and it's not p's turn, p backs off by clearing its flag and entering wait; when turn eventually rotates to p (WaitForTurn), it re-raises its flag. ExitCS hands turn to the other process and clears the flag. MutualExclusion holds because entry to critical requires the other's flag to be FALSE (checked in Check and EnterFromCheck), and the turn variable ensures that when both flags are raised, only the process whose turn it is will persist while the other backs off.

rationale: Readers-writers model with integer reader count and boolean writer flag. The transitions allow a reader to be added while writer is true (first disjunct does not check writer=FALSE), which violates the core readers-writers safety property. No invariant is defined beyond TypeOK. The prompt description is completely empty, making this useless as a training pair. Missing the fundamental invariant: ~(writer /\ readers > 0). Would teach incomplete modeling.

TrafficLight models state transitions but the timer countdown allows timer to go negative (no guard against timer=0 in the stutter step). Only invariant is TypeOK (implicit). No safety property beyond typing. The empty problem description means this example teaches nothing about prompt-to-spec mapping.

The spec conflates producer and consumer roles: a producer that is 'producing' then consumes from the buffer, which is semantically wrong. Only TypeOK as invariant; no buffer-overflow safety or mutual-exclusion property. Empty problem description removes training value for prompt-to-spec learning.

This does not model Dekker's algorithm at all. There is no critical section, no busy-waiting, no turn-based yielding. The spec just resets wants to <<FALSE,FALSE>> in every step. Only 2 distinct states confirms the model is trivially degenerate. Only TypeOK invariant. Would teach a fundamentally incorrect model of mutual exclusion.

Correctly models a binary toggle, but this is maximally trivial: 2 states, 1 variable, no meaningful invariant beyond TypeOK. Empty problem description. No training value as it teaches nothing generalizable.

Correct bounded counter model for parking lot. Clean idiomatic TLA+. However, only TypeOK as invariant - misses obvious safety property like occupied <= N (which TypeOK implies but should be stated explicitly as a domain invariant). Empty problem description. Too simple for meaningful training.
Identical concept to RL003 (LightSwitch) - a binary toggle with 2 states. Only TypeOK, no meaningful invariant. Empty problem description. Duplicate of an already-trivial pattern.

Models a simplified retransmission protocol with send/receive/timeout/giveup actions. However, GiveUp moves to the next chunk instead of failing, which is semantically questionable. Only TypeOK invariant - no safety property about message delivery or data integrity. The channel model is oversimplified (set instead of lossy channel). Empty problem description.

The Next action is flawed: any process i can pass the token regardless of whether it holds it (no guard token=i). The EXCEPT with ![token'] uses a primed variable in the index which is not standard TLA+. No mutual exclusion invariant despite modeling critical sections. Only TypeOK, which incorrectly includes N \in Nat as a type constraint on a CONSTANT.

Models push/pop on a bounded stack. maxCap is initialized nondeterministically but then never changes, which is unusual - should be a CONSTANT. Push always appends 0, losing any notion of varied data. Only TypeOK, no interesting stack property. Extends Sequences but Seq(Nat) in TypeOK is infinite and not checkable by TLC.

Correct 3-state linear state machine (Pending->Running->Done). EXTENDS TLC is unnecessary. Only TypeOK invariant. Too trivial to be useful training data - just a linear sequence of 3 states with no branching, concurrency, or interesting properties.

Well-crafted Klotski puzzle spec with proper piece movement, collision detection, and goal state. Uses advanced TLA+ features (higher-order operators, set comprehensions, CHOOSE). TypeOK is present; KlotskiGoal serves as a reachability property. High complexity (25955 states). Good training value for non-trivial modeling. Prompt is fragmented but spec itself is strong.
chain_of_thought: This is the Klotski sliding puzzle, where the single state variable board holds the current set of pieces (each piece being a set of grid positions). The board is a set of Piece values where Piece = SUBSET Pos and Pos = (0..W-1) x (0..H-1) for a 4x5 grid. The key operators are: dir(p, es) computes valid von Neumann neighbor directions from position p that lead to occupied (non-empty) cells, filtering out directions that go off-grid or land on other empty squares. move(p, d) finds the piece adjacent to empty position p in direction d using ChooseOne, then translates that entire piece in the opposite direction (toward the empty space). update(e, es) combines these: for each valid direction from empty cell e, it computes the moved piece and validates that the translated piece does not collide with other pieces and stays within bounds, then constructs new board configurations. Init sets the Klotski starting layout (10 pieces including the 2x2 block, four 1x2 blocks, one 2x1 block, and four 1x1 blocks). Next existentially picks an empty cell and transitions to any valid board in update(e, empty). The state space is finite because the grid is 4x5=20 cells with a fixed number of pieces.

rationale: Same spec as train_6 (SlidingPuzzles/Klotski). Identical content - this is a duplicate. While the spec itself is good, having two identical entries wastes training budget. Downgraded training_value for redundancy.
chain_of_thought: This is a completion variant of the Klotski sliding puzzle specification. The board variable holds the current piece configuration as a set of sets of positions on a 4x5 grid. The ChooseOne operator is critical -- it selects the unique piece from board that contains a given position, enabling move(p,d) to identify which piece to slide. The dir operator filters the four cardinal directions to those that are in-bounds and point to occupied cells (not in the empty set es), ensuring we only attempt to move pieces that actually exist adjacent to the gap. The move operator translates the identified piece by the negation of direction d (sliding the piece toward the empty space). The update operator validates moves by checking that the translated piece does not overlap with remaining pieces and stays within Pos bounds, then constructs the resulting board by removing the old piece and inserting the moved one. KlotskiGoal checks whether the 2x2 block has reached the bottom-center exit position. The specification explores all reachable board configurations via BFS when checked with TLC, and the goal predicate can be used as an invariant negation to find the solution path.

rationale: Excellent readers-writers spec with queue-based fairness. Has both Safety invariant (mutual exclusion between readers/writers, at most one writer) and Liveness temporal properties. Uses WF fairness conditions. Well-structured with clear action decomposition. High state count (21527) confirms non-trivial model. Strong training example.
chain_of_thought: The readers-writers problem with fair queuing requires three state variables: readers (set of currently reading actors), writers (set of currently writing actors), and waiting (a sequence of tagged requests). The waiting queue is a sequence of pairs like <<"read", actor>> or <<"write", actor>>, which is essential for FIFO fairness -- a set would lose ordering. Helper operators WaitingToRead and WaitingToWrite extract actor IDs from the queue using SelectSeq filtering. TryRead/TryWrite append to the queue (guarded by not already waiting). ReadOrWrite processes the head of the queue: if it is a read request, the actor joins readers (multiple concurrent readers allowed); if it is a write request, it requires readers = {} (exclusive access). The writers = {} precondition on ReadOrWrite prevents new reads or writes while a writer is active. StopActivity models a reader or writer finishing. The Safety invariant has two parts: readers and writers cannot be simultaneously non-empty (no read-write overlap), and at most one writer exists. These are sufficient because ReadOrWrite only grants write access when readers is empty, and Write only adds one actor to writers at a time. The Fairness conditions using WF ensure liveness -- every actor eventually reads and writes.

rationale: Identical spec to train_22 (ReadersWriters). Same high quality but is a duplicate entry. Reduced training_value due to redundancy.
chain_of_thought: This is a completion variant of the readers-writers specification with FIFO queuing. The three state variables -- readers (set), writers (set), and waiting (sequence of tagged pairs) -- separate the concern of who is currently active from who is waiting. The sequence type for waiting is critical: it enforces FIFO ordering so that a write request is not starved by later read requests jumping ahead. The ToSet helper converts the sequence to a set for filtering operations via SelectSeq. The key design decision is that ReadOrWrite dispatches on Head(waiting) with writers = {} as a global precondition -- this means no new access is granted while any writer is active, preventing read-write races. The Read action does not require readers = {} because multiple concurrent readers are safe. The Write action additionally requires readers = {} because a writer needs exclusive access. The StopActivity action uses an IF to dispatch between reader removal and writer removal. The Safety invariant captures both the mutual exclusion between readers and writers and the single-writer constraint, which together ensure the standard readers-writers safety properties are maintained.

rationale: Classic Lamport DieHard water jug spec - textbook quality. Excellent comments explaining every decision. TypeOK + NotSolved as reachability check. All six actions correctly modeled with proper Min helper. Exemplary idiomatic TLA+ with thorough documentation. Outstanding training example.
chain_of_thought: The Die Hard water jug puzzle needs exactly two state variables: big (gallons in the 5-gallon jug) and small (gallons in the 3-gallon jug). These two integers fully describe the system state because the jugs and the faucet (infinite source/sink) are the only containers. TypeOK bounds big to 0..5 and small to 0..3, giving 6*4=24 possible states -- a very small state space ideal for exhaustive BFS by TLC. The six actions model all physically meaningful operations: FillSmallJug and FillBigJug model filling from the faucet (setting to capacity); EmptySmallJug and EmptyBigJug model pouring onto the ground (setting to 0); SmallToBig and BigToSmall model pouring between jugs, using Min to compute the result -- the receiving jug gets the lesser of its capacity and the total water in both jugs, and the donor loses exactly what the receiver gained. The Init state has both jugs empty. The invariant NotSolved (big != 4) is intentionally the negation of the goal: TLC's counterexample trace when this invariant is violated reveals the shortest sequence of actions to reach big = 4, effectively solving the puzzle via model checking.

rationale: Identical to train_40 (DieHard). Same textbook-quality spec but is a duplicate. Reduced training_value for redundancy.
chain_of_thought: The problem requires modeling water-pouring between two jugs of known capacity (3-gallon and 5-gallon) to measure exactly 4 gallons. Two state variables suffice: big (0..5) and small (0..3), tracking gallons in each jug; no other hidden state exists because jugs are uncalibrated so only full, empty, or pour-until-limit operations are meaningful. The state space is bounded because big and small are each constrained to their respective capacity ranges, giving at most 6*4=24 reachable states. Six actions model every physically distinct operation: fill either jug from the faucet (set to capacity), empty either jug (set to 0), or pour from one to the other where the target becomes Min(total, capacity) and the source loses exactly what the target gained, preserving the water conservation invariant big+small = big'+small' across a pour. The negated goal NotSolved == big # 4 is checked as an invariant so TLC's breadth-first search produces a counterexample trace that IS the shortest solution; TypeOK (small in 0..3, big in 0..5) is the safety invariant confirming that no action can overflow or underflow a jug.

rationale: Conway's Game of Life in TLA+ - creative use of the formalism for cellular automata. Recursive Sum, neighbor scoring, and grid update are well-implemented. However, only TypeOK invariant - no interesting properties about the automaton are specified. Good training value for showing TLA+ can model non-distributed systems. 512 states with N presumably small.
chain_of_thought: Conway's Game of Life on an NxN grid requires a single state variable grid mapping each position <<x,y>> in 1..N x 1..N to a BOOLEAN (alive/dead), since the next generation is a pure function of the current grid. The state space is bounded by 2^(N^2) configurations. A recursive Sum operator over a function and set is needed to count live neighbors; the sc function maps the extended domain (0..N+1)^2 to 0 or 1, returning 0 for out-of-bounds or dead cells and 1 for live in-bounds cells, which cleanly handles boundary conditions without special-casing edges. The score operator computes the neighbor count by summing sc over the 8 adjacent offsets for each position. The Next relation is deterministic: each cell's new value follows the standard rules (alive with 2 or 3 neighbors stays alive, dead with exactly 3 becomes alive, all others die), expressed as a single function update grid' = [p in Pos |-> ...]. Init allows any Boolean grid, making the spec a universal step relation for all possible Game of Life evolutions.

rationale: Simple wrapping counter. MAX should be a CONSTANT, not a variable that never changes - this is a TLA+ anti-pattern. Extends Sequences and FiniteSets unnecessarily. Only TypeOK invariant. Too simple and teaches the bad habit of using variables for constants.

Better than gold_cache/SYN003 - properly models timer countdown with explicit guards for timer=0 transitions. Clear state machine logic. However, still only TypeOK invariant - misses properties like 'Yellow always follows Green' or cycle correctness. Extends Sequences and FiniteSets unnecessarily. Acceptable but not outstanding.
chain_of_thought: A traffic light cycling through Red, Green, Yellow requires two state variables: state (the current color) and timer (a countdown integer). The timer is necessary because each color phase has a distinct duration (Red=60, Green=30, Yellow=5), and the transition to the next color only fires when the timer reaches 0. The state space is bounded: state has 3 values and timer ranges over 0..100, yielding at most 303 reachable states. Each of the six disjuncts in Next models either a timer tick (state unchanged, timer decrements by 1) or a color transition (timer=0 triggers the cyclic successor Red->Green->Yellow->Red with the appropriate new timer value). TypeOK (state in {Red,Green,Yellow} /\ timer in 0..100) is the safety invariant; it is sufficient because the Init fixes state=Red, timer=60, and every action either decrements within range or resets to a known constant, so no action can produce an out-of-range value.

rationale: Clean circular buffer model with read/write pointers and occupancy tracking via binary flags. Pointer wraparound is correct. However, only TypeOK invariant - missing obvious properties like read pointer never overtakes write pointer, or buffer consistency. Data is just 0/1 flags rather than actual values, simplifying the model significantly.
chain_of_thought: A circular buffer of size N requires three state variables: buffer (an array of N slots tracking occupancy), readPtr, and writePtr (both indices into 1..N). The buffer array uses 0/1 values rather than actual data because the spec focuses on the structural protocol, not payload. The state space is bounded: buffer in [1..N -> {0,1}] has 2^N configurations, and each pointer is in 1..N. The Write action guards on buffer[writePtr]=0 (slot empty), sets it to 1, and advances writePtr modulo N via the IF writePtr=N THEN 1 ELSE writePtr+1 idiom; Read symmetrically guards on buffer[readPtr]=1, clears the slot, and advances readPtr. These guards enforce the key safety property that a write never overwrites unread data and a read never reads an empty slot. The circular wrap-around is achieved by the conditional reset to 1 instead of using modular arithmetic, which avoids importing additional operators.

rationale: Good dining philosophers model with proper Left/Right fork helpers and 3-phase state machine (thinking->hungry->eating). Atomic fork pickup avoids deadlock by design. However, only TypeOK invariant - critically missing the mutual exclusion property (no two adjacent philosophers eat simultaneously) and deadlock-freedom. Fork ownership range 0..N is correct.
chain_of_thought: The dining philosophers problem for N philosophers seated in a ring requires two state variables: forks (an array mapping each fork index 1..N to 0 or the philosopher holding it) and states (an array mapping each philosopher to thinking/hungry/eating). Two variables suffice because fork ownership and philosopher phase completely determine what actions are available. The state space is bounded: forks in [1..N -> 0..N] and states in [1..N -> {thinking,hungry,eating}]. Left(i) and Right(i) compute the circular neighbors, with Left wrapping 1 to N and Right wrapping N to 1. Three actions per philosopher model the lifecycle: Hungry(i) transitions from thinking to hungry without touching forks; PickUp(i) atomically acquires both adjacent forks only if both are free (preventing partial acquisition and thus avoiding a specific class of deadlock); PutDown(i) releases both forks and returns to thinking. The existential quantifier \E i \in 1..N in Next models nondeterministic interleaving. Note the spec deliberately allows global deadlock (all hungry, all forks taken) since no fairness is imposed.

rationale: Buggy parking lot model. The 'occupied' set and 'cars' counter are redundant and inconsistent: entering adds cars+1 to occupied but cars might not match set cardinality after leaves. Leave removes element 'cars' which may not be the one that entered. With only 4 distinct states, the model is clearly degenerate. Only TypeOK invariant.

Does not model Dekker's algorithm. No critical section variable, no process control flow, no busy-waiting loop. The Next relation just nondeterministically changes wants flags - there is no connection to mutual exclusion. Only 3 distinct states confirms this is a trivially broken model. Would actively teach incorrect mutual exclusion patterns.

The prompt describes a spec with a TypeOK violation, and the response IS the buggy spec unchanged. inCS is set to token (the old value) on every step regardless of which process acts. The TypeOK violation is expected since inCS cycles through 1..N but the invariant says inCS \in 1..N which should hold - the real bug is semantic (inCS has no relation to critical section entry). Useless as training data since it reinforces the bug.
Exact duplicate of train_81 - same buggy TokenRing spec presented as a response to the same error feedback. Still contains the original bug. Duplicate of an already-rejected spec.

Completely wrong leader election model. Declares IDs constant but never uses it. 'leader' and 'active' just cycle through 1..N deterministically with no comparison of process IDs, no message passing, no election logic whatsoever. Only 3 distinct states. This would teach a fundamentally broken model of leader election.
Same flawed TokenRing as gold_cache/SYN001. Any process can pass the token without holding it. Uses primed variable in EXCEPT index. No mutual exclusion invariant. Duplicate of already-rejected spec.

Simple bounded counter. The line 'counter' \in 0..MAX' in Next is redundant since the IF already ensures this. Only TypeOK invariant (stated twice, redundantly). Extends Sequences and FiniteSets unnecessarily. Too simple and slightly non-idiomatic.

Better than gold_cache/RL010 - uses two variables (spaces, cars) with conservation invariant spaces+cars=N in TypeOK. However, N is hardcoded as 10 rather than being a CONSTANT, which is non-idiomatic. The Next relation has a precedence issue: the first disjunct's conjuncts may not bind correctly without explicit grouping. Acceptable but has rough edges.

Clean simple counter that increments to MAX and stops. Correct logic. However, the guard 'counter <= MAX' in Next is always true given TypeOK, making it redundant. Only TypeOK invariant. Extends Sequences and FiniteSets unnecessarily. Too trivial for meaningful training.

A trivial clock counter with an unnecessary 'max' variable that just copies the constant MAX. TypeOK uses Int instead of 0..MAX, which is far too loose. The only invariant is a meaningless type check. Extends Sequences and FiniteSets for no reason. Module header has a stray newline. Would teach bad habits: redundant state variables, overly broad type invariants, no meaningful safety property.

The prompt asks for a ticket dispenser where customers take increasing ticket numbers and a counter shows the currently-served number, but the spec increments both ticketNumber and counter simultaneously and independently in each step, so there is no relationship between them. There is no modeling of customers, no queue, no serving logic. The counter should lag behind the ticket number. Only TypeOK is checked, no meaningful safety invariant like counter <= ticketNumber. Too simplistic and semantically wrong.

Models a simple three-state FSM (Pending->Running->Done) which is correct but trivially simple. The 'done' variable is completely redundant since it mirrors status='Done'. No meaningful invariant beyond the implicit TypeOK. The stuttering step at Done is fine but adds no value. Only 3 distinct states means this teaches nothing about TLA+ that a student could not figure out in seconds. Too trivial for training.

Models a bounded stack with push/pop. Init has a subtle issue: Len(stack) <= maxCap is a conjunct in Init but stack is <<>> so it is always true, making it vacuous. Push only ever appends 0, not arbitrary values, which is a simplification but not necessarily wrong. The TypeOK invariant is reasonable but uses Seq(Nat) which is infinite and not checkable by TLC. maxCap is initialized nondeterministically from 1..N which is interesting but the value never changes, so it could just be a constant. Borderline useful.

Critical flaw: the circular buffer has no guard preventing write pointer from overtaking read pointer or vice versa. Reads and writes can happen freely, so the buffer can overflow or underflow. There is no count of items in the buffer, no fullness/emptiness tracking. The only invariant is TypeOK. A circular buffer's key safety property (no overflow/underflow) is entirely missing. The read operation blindly advances the read pointer even if the buffer is empty. Would teach incorrect circular buffer design.

Fundamentally broken leader election. Does not model a ring of processes at all: there is a single 'state' counter that cycles 1..N and sets leader=ID when state=N. ID is a constant, not per-process. There are no messages, no message passing, no comparison of IDs. TypeOK constrains leader and state to 1..N but these are meaningless. This would actively teach wrong patterns for distributed algorithms. Only 6 distinct states for a leader election is a red flag.

Correctly models a toggle switch alternating ON/OFF. However, it extends TLC module unnecessarily and has a non-standard comment syntax (--@@). Only 2 distinct states, no meaningful invariant beyond TypeOK. This is the simplest possible TLA+ spec and teaches essentially nothing. A toggle switch is too trivial to be useful training data.

Reasonable structure for a bounded retransmission protocol with Send, Receive, Timeout, and GiveUp actions. However, GiveUp moves to next chunk without clearing the channel, which is semantically odd. Message loss is not explicitly modeled as nondeterministic drop - instead Timeout keeps the channel unchanged. The channel is modeled as a set which is fine. Only TypeOK invariant, no meaningful safety property like 'all chunks eventually delivered in order' or 'retransmit count bounded'. The Receive action uses CHOOSE on a singleton set which works but is non-idiomatic. Missing key safety invariants.

This is Lamport's classic DieHard water jug problem spec. Perfectly correct, beautifully commented, idiomatic TLA+ throughout. All six actions are properly defined. The Min helper is clean. TypeOK is tight (0..3, 0..5). NotSolved as an invariant to find solutions is a clever and instructive TLC usage pattern. The SmallToBig/BigToSmall use of primed variables in the same action is advanced idiomatic TLA+. Excellent training example that teaches commenting style, action decomposition, and creative TLC usage.
chain_of_thought: The structured description identifies variables big and small with 7 action-level operators, which correspond to the six jug operations (FillSmallJug, FillBigJug, EmptySmallJug, EmptyBigJug, SmallToBig, BigToSmall) plus the Min helper. The two integer variables big in 0..5 and small in 0..3 model the gallons in each jug; these ranges bound the state space to 24 states. Each fill action sets a jug to its capacity while leaving the other unchanged; each empty action sets a jug to 0. The pour actions use Min to model the physical constraint that you either fill the target or empty the source: SmallToBig sets big' = Min(big+small, 5) and computes small' as the remainder, preserving total water. The TypeOK invariant (small in 0..3, big in 0..5) confirms capacity is never exceeded. The NotSolved == big # 4 predicate is designed to be violated by TLC's model checker, producing a counterexample trace that constitutes the puzzle's solution via breadth-first search.

rationale: An exemplary abstract specification of synchronous termination detection from Stephan Merz. Models node termination, wakeup, and detection with elegant use of nondeterminism (terminationDetected' \in {terminationDetected, terminated'}). Has three meaningful properties: TDCorrect (safety), Quiescence, and Liveness. Uses WF_vars(DetectTermination) for fairness. Proper ASSUME, clean operator definitions. Teaches advanced patterns: refinement specs, nondeterministic initial states, fairness, liveness properties. One of the best possible training examples.
chain_of_thought: This abstract specification of termination detection in a ring requires two state variables: active (a function from Node to BOOLEAN tracking whether each node is still computing) and terminationDetected (a BOOLEAN flag). The terminated predicate is defined as the universal quantification that no node is active. The state space is bounded: active has 2^N configurations and terminationDetected is Boolean, giving 2^(N+1) states. Three actions model the protocol: Terminate(i) deactivates node i and nondeterministically may update terminationDetected to reflect the new termination status (using set membership in {terminationDetected, terminated'} to allow but not require detection); Wakeup(i,j) allows any active node to reactivate another, preventing premature detection; DetectTermination fires only when all nodes are inactive and sets the flag to TRUE. Weak fairness WF_vars(DetectTermination) ensures that if termination persists, detection eventually occurs, enabling the liveness property terminated ~> terminationDetected. The safety invariant TDCorrect (terminationDetected => terminated) ensures no false positive detection.

rationale: A correct Game of Life implementation in TLA+. Uses RECURSIVE Sum which is advanced. The sc function cleverly handles boundary conditions. The score function computes neighbor counts correctly. Init is nondeterministic over all possible grids. However, no meaningful invariant is defined (only TypeOK implied). The CASE construct for boundary handling is somewhat unusual. The deterministic Next (grid is fully determined by current state) means this is really a function, not a concurrent system, which is a slightly unusual use of TLA+. Still valuable as it demonstrates RECURSIVE, function definitions, and complex state.
chain_of_thought: The structured description specifies a single variable grid over constant N with one action-level and one temporal-level operator. The grid maps positions <<x,y>> in 1..N x 1..N to BOOLEAN, giving a bounded state space of 2^(N^2). The recursive Sum operator is needed to count live neighbors by summing over a finite set, using CHOOSE to peel off elements one at a time. The sc function extends the grid domain to (0..N+1)^2, returning 0 for any out-of-bounds or dead cell and 1 for live in-bounds cells; this elegantly avoids boundary-condition special cases in the neighbor count. The score function computes the 8-neighbor sum by translating each offset and looking up sc. Next is a single deterministic function update applying Conway's rules: a cell is alive next step iff it is currently alive with 2-3 neighbors or currently dead with exactly 3 neighbors. Init allows any Boolean assignment, so TLC explores all possible initial configurations.

rationale: Excellent PCR (DNA amplification) model with well-thought-out chemistry. Has meaningful invariants: TypeOK, preservationInvariant (conservation of matter), and a liveness property. The nondeterministic anneal action (\E k \in 1..natMin) is elegant. Comments are thorough and explanatory. The temperature-driven state machine (Hot->TooHot->Hot->Warm->Hot) correctly models the PCR cycle. Minor idiom issue: Spec lacks fairness so primerDepleted liveness won't hold (noted in comments). A creative, domain-specific example that teaches conservation invariants and scientific modeling.
chain_of_thought: The PCR specification models DNA amplification through temperature-driven phase transitions, requiring five state variables: tee (temperature phase as a string), primer (count of available primers), dna (double-stranded DNA count), template (single-stranded template count), and hybrid (primer-template hybrid count). The temperature variable tee cycles through Hot->TooHot->Hot->Warm->Hot, acting as a phase controller that gates which biochemical action can fire. The heat action denatures all dna and hybrids into templates at TooHot, recovering primers from hybrids. The cool action simply transitions from TooHot to Hot. The anneal action nondeterministically chooses k in 1..natMin(primer,template) primers to bind to templates, forming k hybrids; the existential quantifier models the stochastic nature of annealing. The extend action converts all hybrids to dna at the Hot temperature. The conservation invariant template + primer + 2*(dna + hybrid) = PRIMER + 2*DNA holds because every action preserves the total count of DNA building blocks, providing a key safety check. The state space is bounded by the constants DNA and PRIMER since no action creates matter.

rationale: Stephan Merz's N-Queens PlusCal specification, a classic from the TLA+ examples repository. Combines PlusCal algorithm with TLA+ translation. Has excellent invariants: TypeInvariant, Invariant (sols subset of Solutions, and completeness when todo is empty), plus NoSolutions for TLC exploration. Includes fairness (WF_vars) and Liveness/LiveSpec. The Attacks predicate, IsSolution, and Solutions set are textbook-quality. Teaches PlusCal-to-TLA+ translation, set comprehension, and correctness proof structure.
chain_of_thought: The N-Queens spec combines a declarative problem definition with a PlusCal-translated iterative solver. The Attacks predicate checks three conditions (same column, same diagonal, same anti-diagonal) between any two queen positions, and IsSolution verifies no pair attacks. The algorithm state requires three variables: todo (a set of partial queen-placement sequences to explore), sols (accumulated full solutions), and pc (the PlusCal program counter). The state space is bounded because partial solutions are sequences over 1..N of length at most N, giving at most N^N possible sequences. The single action nxtQ picks any partial solution from todo, computes all valid column extensions for the next row by filtering columns that don't attack existing queens, and either adds them to sols (if length reaches N) or to todo (otherwise). The key invariant is sols \subseteq Solutions /\ (todo={} => Solutions \subseteq sols), ensuring partial correctness: sols only contains valid solutions, and upon termination all solutions have been found. Weak fairness WF_vars(nxtQ) in LiveSpec guarantees the algorithm terminates when todo becomes empty.

rationale: A sophisticated sliding block puzzle (Klotski) specification with 25955 distinct states. The ChooseOne, dir, move, and update operators are well-structured. Models piece movement with collision detection. KlotskiGoal checks if the 2x2 block reached the exit. However, TypeOK is weak (just board in SUBSET Piece) and there is no Spec formula defined (missing temporal formula). The W==4 H==5 on one line is non-standard formatting. Despite minor issues, this is a complex, real-world puzzle model that teaches set operations, CHOOSE, and spatial reasoning in TLA+.
chain_of_thought: The sliding puzzle (Klotski) spec models a board as a set of pieces, where each piece is a set of <<x,y>> positions on a 4x5 grid. The single variable board (a set of sets of positions) suffices because piece identity is determined by position occupancy. The state space is bounded by the fixed grid dimensions (W=4, H=5, so 20 positions) and the fixed number of pieces. The Init fixes the board to the standard Klotski configuration with 10 pieces of varying sizes. The Next action identifies empty positions (Pos \ UNION board), then for each empty position computes possible moves via the dir operator (filtering von Neumann neighbors that are not also empty) and the move operator (finding which piece occupies the adjacent cell and translating it). The update function filters moves to those that stay within bounds and don't overlap other pieces, then produces new board states by swapping the piece with its translated version. The KlotskiGoal checks that the 2x2 block is no longer at its initial position, and TLC's counterexample trace yields the solution sequence.

rationale: Clean mutual exclusion spec with idle->trying->critical->idle transitions. The MutualExclusion invariant is the key safety property and is correctly defined. TypeOK is precise. The Enter action properly checks no other process is critical. Well-structured with named actions per phase. Could be improved by adding fairness or a starvation-freedom liveness property, and the model is somewhat simplified (no actual contention mechanism like flags or turns), but as a training example it teaches the core mutual exclusion pattern clearly.
chain_of_thought: Mutual exclusion for N processes requires a single state variable pc mapping each process in 1..N to one of three states: idle, trying, or critical. This three-phase model is the minimal abstraction capturing the essential protocol structure. The state space is bounded at 3^N configurations. TryEnter(p) moves process p from idle to trying; Enter(p) moves from trying to critical but only if no other process is in critical (the universal guard \A q \in Procs : q # p => pc[q] # "critical"); Exit(p) returns from critical to idle. The safety invariant MutualExclusion states that no two distinct processes are simultaneously critical, expressed as a universal quantification over all pairs. This invariant is sufficient because Enter's guard directly enforces it: a process can only enter critical when the invariant already holds for all other processes, and Exit only removes a process from critical.

rationale: Thorough two-phase commit specification with all key phases: Prepare, VoteYes/No, DecideCommit/Abort, CommitPart/AbortPart, Done. The Consistency invariant (committed participant implies commit decision) is meaningful and correct. All variables are properly constrained in TypeOK. The coordinator-participant interaction is well-modeled. Minor issue: AbortPart allows aborting a 'working' participant which might be debatable. Good training value as 2PC is a fundamental distributed systems protocol.
chain_of_thought: Two-phase commit requires three state variables: coordState (the coordinator's phase: init/waiting/committed/aborted), partState (a function mapping each participant to working/prepared/aborted/committed), and decision (none/commit/abort recording the global outcome). The coordinator drives the protocol: Prepare moves it from init to waiting, triggering the voting phase. Each participant independently votes yes (VoteYes sets partState to prepared) or no (VoteNo sets to aborted). DecideCommit fires only when ALL participants are prepared (the universal guard), setting decision to commit; DecideAbort fires when ANY participant aborted (the existential guard). CommitPart and AbortPart propagate the decision to individual participants. The state space is bounded: coordState has 4 values, partState has 4^N configurations, decision has 3 values. The Consistency invariant (partState[p]="committed" => decision="commit") ensures no participant commits unless the coordinator decided commit, which is sufficient because DecideCommit is the only action that enables CommitPart, and DecideCommit requires all-prepared.

rationale: Solid Bakery algorithm implementation with doorway, waiting, and critical sections. MutualExclusion invariant is the correct safety property. The Max operator, flag/num arrays, and process ID tie-breaking (p < q) are all correctly modeled. The artificial bound of num < 18 and num \in 0..20 is a pragmatic TLC bound but slightly awkward. The two-phase doorway (set flag then unset) is correct per the algorithm. Minor idiom issue: Max uses CHOOSE which is fine but non-standard compared to a recursive definition. Good training example for classic concurrent algorithms.
chain_of_thought: Lamport's bakery algorithm requires three state variables: num (ticket numbers, bounded 0..20 to keep TLC's state space finite), flag (a Boolean array indicating a process is in the doorway choosing a ticket), and pc (the program counter tracking idle/doorway/waiting/critical). The Doorway action sets flag[p]=TRUE and assigns num[p] = Max(all tickets)+1, guarded by Max < 18 to prevent overflow of the bounded ticket range. FinishDoorway clears the flag and transitions to waiting. EnterCS checks two conditions against every other process: the other's flag is false (not mid-doorway) AND either the other has no ticket (num[q]=0) or the current process has strict priority (lower ticket, breaking ties by process ID). This two-condition check is the crux of the bakery algorithm's correctness. ExitCS resets the ticket to 0 and returns to idle. The MutualExclusion invariant holds because two processes in critical would both need to see the other's ticket as non-threatening, but the ticket ordering and the tie-breaking rule make this impossible.

rationale: Models a key-value store with Put/Get. The Linearizability invariant is not actually linearizability - it checks lastGet[k] = store[k] or lastGet[k] = 0, which just says reads return current or initial value. True linearizability would require tracking operation history. The spec is also overly simple: a single-server sequential store is trivially linearizable. There is no concurrency, no network, no client modeling. The invariant name is misleading. Acceptable but could teach students a wrong understanding of linearizability.
chain_of_thought: A linearizable key-value store requires two state variables: store (the actual server-side mapping from Keys to Values union {0}) and lastGet (a shadow mapping recording the most recent Get result for each key). The store variable is necessary as the ground truth; lastGet is necessary to state the linearizability property. The state space is bounded: both are functions from 1..N to 1..N union {0}, giving (N+1)^N configurations each. Put(k,v) updates store[k] to v, leaving lastGet unchanged; Get(k) reads the current store[k] into lastGet[k], leaving store unchanged. The Linearizability invariant states lastGet[k] = store[k] \/ lastGet[k] = 0 for all keys, meaning a Get always returns either the most recent Put value or the initial value. This is sufficient because Get atomically snapshots store[k], and no concurrent interleaving can cause lastGet to reflect a stale non-initial value since each Get reads the current store in a single atomic step.

rationale: Attempts single-decree Paxos but has issues. The Accept action's value selection is too permissive: it allows any value in promisedVals rather than requiring the value from the highest-numbered ballot. This breaks the Paxos invariant that the chosen value must be safe. The Consistency invariant only checks chosen is in Values, not that once chosen it stays the same. A true Paxos spec needs the 'chosen value never changes' invariant. The Quorum definition using Cardinality is correct. Missing the crucial safety property. Borderline because the structure is right but semantics are subtly wrong.
chain_of_thought: Single-decree Paxos requires four state variables: maxBal (highest ballot each acceptor has promised), maxVBal (highest ballot at which each acceptor accepted a value), maxVal (the value accepted at maxVBal), and chosen (the globally chosen value, or -1). The quorum definition {Q in SUBSET Acceptors : Cardinality(Q)*2 > N} ensures any two quorums intersect, which is the foundation of Paxos safety. Prepare(b) gets a quorum to promise ballot b by checking maxBal[a] < b for all a in Q, then updating their maxBal. Accept(b,v) requires a quorum where all members have maxBal=b (they promised this ballot), and constrains v to either be free (if no prior accepted values exist in the quorum) or match a previously accepted value. Choose fires when a quorum unanimously holds the same value, recording it in chosen. The state space is bounded by Ballots=0..4, Values={1,2}, and N acceptors. The Consistency invariant (chosen # -1 => chosen in Values) is a basic type check; the deeper safety property that chosen never changes is ensured structurally because Choose requires chosen=-1 as a precondition.

rationale: Well-structured snapshot isolation model. Begin takes a snapshot, Write tracks write sets, Commit checks for write-write conflicts against already-committed transactions. The NoWriteConflict invariant captures the core SI guarantee. The conflict check in Commit correctly compares against committed transactions. Minor issues: the commit action writes txSnapshot[t][k]+1 rather than a nondeterministic value, which is a simplification. Also, committed transactions' write sets are never cleared, which is correct for conflict detection. Good training value for database isolation concepts.
chain_of_thought: Snapshot isolation requires four state variables: db (the committed database state), txState (each transaction's lifecycle phase), txSnapshot (the read snapshot captured at Begin), and txWrites (the set of keys written by each transaction). txSnapshot is essential because snapshot isolation means reads see a consistent point-in-time view, not the current db. The state space is bounded: db in [Keys -> 0..5], txState has 4 values per transaction, txSnapshot mirrors db's type, and txWrites is a subset of Keys. Begin(t) captures the current db as the transaction's snapshot. Write(t,k) adds k to the write set without modifying db (writes are buffered). Commit(t) checks the write-write conflict condition: no already-committed transaction may have written to any key in txWrites[t]; if clear, it applies the writes to db. Abort(t) discards the transaction. The NoWriteConflict invariant (no two committed transactions share a written key) is sufficient for snapshot isolation correctness because the Commit guard explicitly checks this condition before allowing the state transition to committed.

rationale: The clock sync model is oversimplified. Each node syncs exactly once with one arbitrary other node using integer division averaging, then stops. The ClockBound invariant uses -5..5 which is trivially satisfied for clocks in 0..3. The spec does not model rounds, does not guarantee convergence, and the one-shot sync is unrealistic. Integer division loses precision. After sync, there is no continued drift or re-sync. The epsilon bound should be tight and derived from the protocol, not a loose constant. Too simplified to teach clock synchronization properly.

Clean memory allocator spec with Allocate and Release actions. SafeAllocation invariant correctly prevents double-allocation. The use of free set plus per-client allocated sets is idiomatic. TypeOK properly constrains both variables. Could add an invariant that free and allocated are disjoint and their union equals Pages, but SafeAllocation is the key property. Well-structured, clear, and teaches resource allocation patterns. Good training example.
chain_of_thought: A memory page allocator requires two state variables: free (the set of unallocated pages) and allocated (a function mapping each client to its set of held pages). Two variables are necessary because free could be derived from allocated, but maintaining it explicitly makes the Allocate/Release guards efficient and clear. The state space is bounded: free is a subset of 1..N (2^N possibilities) and allocated maps N clients to subsets of 1..N. Allocate(c) nondeterministically picks a page from free, removes it from free, and adds it to allocated[c]; Release(c) does the reverse. The SafeAllocation invariant (for all distinct clients c1, c2: allocated[c1] \cap allocated[c2] = {}) ensures no page is double-allocated. This is sufficient because Allocate only draws from free (which is disjoint from all allocated sets by construction), so a page entering one client's set cannot already be in another's.

rationale: Good pub-sub broker model with Subscribe, Publish, Deliver actions. The DeliveryGuarantee invariant (delivered <= published for subscribed topics) is correct but could be stronger: it does not guarantee eventual delivery. The model correctly tracks per-subscriber per-topic delivery counts. MaxMsgs=1 limits the state space reasonably. The Done action is slightly odd as it only checks published is at max, not that delivery is complete. Overall a solid example of a message-passing system with meaningful invariants.
chain_of_thought: A publish-subscribe broker requires three state variables: subscriptions (mapping each subscriber to its set of subscribed topics), published (a counter per topic tracking how many messages have been published), and delivered (a matrix of counters tracking how many messages each subscriber has received per topic). The state space is bounded by MaxMsgs=1 capping published counts and the finite sets of subscribers and topics. Subscribe(s,t) adds topic t to subscriber s's subscription set. Publish(t) increments the published counter for topic t, guarded by the MaxMsgs cap. Deliver(s,t) increments delivered[s][t] only if t is in s's subscriptions and delivered[s][t] < published[t], ensuring delivery does not outpace publication. The DeliveryGuarantee invariant states that for every subscriber-topic pair where the subscriber is registered, delivered count never exceeds published count. This is sufficient because the Deliver guard directly enforces the less-than precondition, making overdelivery impossible.

rationale: Well-implemented Dekker's algorithm with all key phases: set flag, check other's flag, wait/defer, enter critical section, exit. The MutualExclusion invariant is the essential safety property. The Other(p) helper is clean. The Deadlock_Freedom invariant is interesting though it is a state predicate, not a liveness property. The multi-phase protocol with flag clearing and turn-based deference is correctly modeled. A good training example for classic mutual exclusion algorithms with more detail than the simpler BM001.
chain_of_thought: Dekker's algorithm for 2 processes requires three state variables: wants (a Boolean flag per process indicating intent to enter the critical section), turn (an integer in {0,1} breaking symmetry when both want access), and pc (a 6-state program counter per process tracking idle/set_flag/check/wait/critical/exit). The algorithm proceeds as: SetFlag raises the process's flag and moves to set_flag; Check tests the other's flag and either enters critical (if other's flag is down) or moves to check; Wait handles contention by yielding (clearing its own flag and waiting for its turn), then re-raising its flag; ExitCS clears the flag and transfers turn to the other process. The turn variable is Dekker's key innovation over naive flag-based approaches, preventing livelock when both processes simultaneously raise flags. The MutualExclusion invariant ~(pc[0]="critical" /\ pc[1]="critical") holds because entering critical requires the other's flag to be FALSE, but a process in critical always has its flag TRUE, creating a contradiction. The state space is bounded: 2 Boolean flags, 2 turn values, and 6^2 pc combinations yield at most 288 states.

rationale: Extremely oversimplified: models a packet router as a single integer counter. No packets, no routing, no forwarding logic, no source/destination, no network topology. SafetyBounded is identical to TypeOK. The UNCHANGED vars in Next allows infinite stuttering which hides deadlock. This is a bounded counter, not a packet router. The prompt asks for something far more complex than what is delivered. Would teach students that 'modeling' means counting.

Another single-counter model masquerading as a memory allocator. No blocks, no clients, no allocation tracking, no free list. All three invariants (TypeOK, SafetyBounded, NoOverflow) say essentially the same thing: allocated is in 0..Max. Redundant invariants teach bad practices. The prompt asks to 'track allocated count and ensure it never exceeds capacity' but there is no notion of which blocks are allocated to whom. Trivially simple and semantically hollow.

Models round-robin as a counter modulo N plus a dispatch count. No servers, no requests, no load distribution tracking, no server health. The bound of N*N dispatches is arbitrary. SafetyBounded and SafetyValid are trivially implied by TypeOK. Only one action (Dispatch) means this is a deterministic counter, not a load balancer. Does not model what happens at each server. Too abstract to teach load balancing concepts.

Better than the other manual3 specs: models active threads and queued tasks with Submit, StartTask, FinishTask. The interaction between queued and active is correct. However, still uses only counters (no actual task identity, no thread identity). SafetyBounded and SafetyValid are again trivially implied by TypeOK. Missing meaningful invariants like 'active + queued equals total submitted minus completed'. The UNCHANGED vars stuttering step is questionable. Borderline - has some structure but is too abstract.

Fundamentally confused model: uses a single 'entries' counter and a single 'ttl' counter, but a DNS cache has multiple entries each with their own TTL. A single TTL for all entries is wrong. CacheEntry sets ttl=Max but this overwrites the TTL even if other entries exist. Expire removes an entry only when ttl=0 but there is one TTL for all entries. Refresh resets TTL for all entries at once. The three invariants are all equivalent. This model would teach an incorrect understanding of caching and TTL.

The best of the manual3 batch. Correctly models priority: PrintLow requires high=0, enforcing high-priority-first. SafetyBounded (high+low<=Max) is a genuine non-trivial invariant. However, still uses counters rather than actual job queues, so there is no job identity or ordering within a priority level. SafetyValid is trivially implied by TypeOK. Could be improved with actual sequences for queues. Borderline useful - the priority mechanism is the one interesting aspect.
chain_of_thought: The problem requires tracking two distinct queues differentiated by priority, so we need two integer state variables `high` and `low` representing job counts at each priority level rather than a single queue, because priority scheduling requires knowing how many jobs exist at each level independently. The state space is bounded by the constant Max which caps the total number of jobs (high + low <= Max), giving a finite model-checkable space of (Max+1)^2 states. Four actions model the real-world events: AddHigh and AddLow model job arrival at each priority (guarded by the total capacity constraint), PrintHigh models serving any available high-priority job, and PrintLow models serving a low-priority job only when no high-priority jobs remain (high = 0), which encodes the priority scheduling policy. The safety invariants SafetyBounded (high + low <= Max) and SafetyValid (both counters non-negative) derive directly from the physical constraints that the queue has finite capacity and job counts cannot go negative. These invariants are sufficient because they ensure the system never overflows its buffer and every reachable state has well-defined non-negative counts, while the priority semantics are enforced structurally by PrintLow's guard requiring high = 0.
